proposals.title: Synergistic Potentiation of Host Innate Immunity by Subinhibitory 6-Aminopenicillanic Acid Against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus in a Murine Model
proposals.motivation: The persistent threat of MRSA, complicated by antibiotic resistance and the immunomodulatory effects observed in post-viral (e.g., COVID-19) infection states, necessitates novel therapeutic strategies. Repurposing non-antibacterial agents to disarm pathogens or enhance host defense, as seen with diflunisal, offers a promising avenue to combat resistant infections without directly driving resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subinhibitory concentrations of 6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA), the core β-lactam structure, will synergize with endogenous cathelicidin-related antimicrobial peptides (e.g., LL-37 in humans, mCRAMP in mice) to significantly reduce MRSA burden in vivo by sensitizing the bacterium to innate immune clearance.
proposals.method: We will employ a neutropenic murine thigh infection model with a clinical MRSA strain. Mice will receive systemic treatment with sub-MIC 6-APA alone, intranasal mCRAMP peptide alone, or the combination. Bacterial loads in thigh homogenates will be quantified by CFU enumeration at 24h post-infection. Synergy will be assessed via isobologram analysis. Secondary endpoints will include cytokine profiling (IL-6, TNF-α) and histopathological examination of infected tissues to assess inflammation and immune cell recruitment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for 6-APA as an immunopotentiator, a novel application distinct from its role as a β-lactam precursor. It will define a potential combination therapy strategy that leverages subverted bacterial pathways to restore efficacy of the host's innate immune system, particularly relevant for immunocompromised or post-viral patients.
proposals.novelty: While 6-APA is known to inhibit bacterial cell wall transpeptidases at high concentrations, its use as a subinhibitory synergist with host antimicrobial peptides is unexplored. This approach diverges from direct antibacterial or antivirulence targeting, instead aiming to re-sensitize MRSA to endogenous immune effectors.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of Etamycin in Localized MRSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Models: A Comparative Study with Systemic Infection Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Preclinical evaluation of novel anti-MRSA agents like etamycin, a promising thiopeptide antibiotic, has historically relied on systemic infection models (e.g., bacteremia, sepsis). However, the most common clinical manifestations of MRSA are localized skin and soft tissue infections (SSTIs). The efficacy of an antibiotic can differ dramatically between systemic and localized infection contexts due to factors like tissue penetration, immune cell interaction, and bacterial virulence expression. This gap is critical to address for accurate translational prediction, especially in the post-COVID era where secondary bacterial infections remain a concern.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that etamycin will demonstrate superior efficacy in localized MRSA SSTI models compared to its performance in traditional systemic infection models, due to its potent inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis and potential anti-virulence effects against key SSTI virulence factors.
proposals.method: We will employ established, pre-2026 murine models of MRSA infection. For localized infection, a subcutaneous abscess model using a clinical MRSA USA300 strain will be utilized. For systemic infection, a tail-vein injection bacteremia model will be used. Etamycin treatment will be administered post-infection at varying doses. Primary endpoints will include bacterial burden (CFU quantification from homogenized tissue or organs), abscess size/weight, and histopathological analysis. We will compare dose-response curves and therapeutic windows between the two models. Additionally, we will profile the expression of key virulence genes (e.g., hla, pvl) in bacteria recovered from each model post-treatment using quantitative RT-PCR.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, comparative data on etamycin's efficacy in physiologically relevant localized versus systemic MRSA infections. It will establish a more accurate preclinical efficacy profile for etamycin, directly informing future clinical trial design for SSTI indications. Furthermore, it will contribute a methodological framework for more nuanced preclinical evaluation of anti-staphylococcal agents, moving beyond the primary reliance on systemic models.
proposals.novelty: While etamycin's in vitro activity is known, its in vivo evaluation has been limited to systemic models. This proposal's novelty lies in its direct, head-to-head comparison of efficacy across distinct infection anatomies, explicitly testing the assumption that systemic infection is the primary predictive model for all MRSA disease. This aligns with and extends Dr. Haste's prior work on anti-virulence strategies by examining efficacy in an infection context where virulence factors are paramount.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial to Assess Immune Priming and Clinical Efficacy of a Three-Month Versus Standard Six-Month Infant Vaccination Schedule for S. aureus and Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccines
proposals.motivation: Infants remain vulnerable to severe bacterial infections like S. aureus and S. pneumoniae in the first six months of life, a period where maternal antibodies wane and the standard vaccination schedule has not yet begun. The fixed assumption of a six-month 'critical period' for vaccine responsiveness is a major barrier to earlier protection, leaving a dangerous immunity gap. Drawing a novel analogy from plant 'hardening,' where early, controlled stress induces systemic acquired resistance, we hypothesize that earlier vaccination could similarly prime the infant immune system.
proposals.hypothesis: Vaccination at three months of age, using a combined S. aureus (e.g., NDV-3) and pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV) regimen, will act as an immune-hardening event, inducing a non-inferior or superior long-term antibody response and T-cell memory compared to the standard six-month schedule.
proposals.method: We will conduct a phase II, randomized, observer-blinded, non-inferiority trial in 300 healthy infants. The intervention arm will receive the first dose of investigational S. aureus and licensed PCV vaccines at three months, followed by standard boosters. The control arm follows the standard schedule starting at six months. Primary endpoints are antigen-specific IgG geometric mean titers (GMTs) and opsonophagocytic activity (OPA) titers at 12 months. Secondary endpoints include vaccine-specific memory B-cell and Th1/Th17 T-cell frequencies (via flow cytometry), safety, and breakthrough infection rates (MRSA, invasive pneumococcal disease) tracked through 24 months. Statistical analysis will use a pre-specified non-inferiority margin for GMT ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence testing the 'critical period' assumption in infant vaccinology, potentially redefining the timeline for initiating robust immunization. It directly addresses the clinical themes of S. aureus and post-transplant infection risk by exploring a proactive strategy to build immunity before periods of high vulnerability, such as prior to elective procedures.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in its direct translational analogy from plant systemic acquired resistance to human infant immunization, challenging a decades-old clinical dogma. It uniquely integrates Dr. Haste's expertise in anti-staphylococcal strategies and real-world vaccine effectiveness into a preventative pediatric trial design.

proposals.title: Non-inferiority of Early (3-month) vs. Standard (6-month) Pneumococcal Vaccination in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) recipients are at high risk for severe pneumococcal disease. Current guidelines recommend initiating vaccination at 3-6 months post-transplant, but optimal timing remains uncertain. Earlier vaccination could provide crucial protection during a period of profound vulnerability, potentially reducing infection-related morbidity and mortality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initiating the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) series at 3 months post-allo-HSCT will be non-inferior to initiation at 6 months for key clinical outcomes, including vaccine immunogenicity, incidence of invasive pneumococcal disease, and all-cause pneumonia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Adult allo-HSCT recipients will be randomized 1:1 to receive PCV13 at 3, 4, and 5 months or at 6, 7, and 8 months post-transplant. The primary outcome is the serotype-specific IgG geometric mean concentration (GMC) one month after the third dose, measured by a validated multiplex immunoassay. Secondary outcomes include rates of invasive pneumococcal disease, radiologically confirmed pneumonia, and hospitalization. Non-inferiority for immunogenicity will be declared if the lower bound of the 95% confidence interval for the GMC ratio (3-month/6-month) is >0.5 for at least 10 of 13 serotypes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to guide the timing of pneumococcal vaccination in this immunocompromised population. It could directly inform clinical practice, potentially leading to revised guidelines that offer earlier protection without compromising efficacy. The findings will also advance understanding of immune reconstitution kinetics post-transplant.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work characterized vaccine response at standard timings, this proposal directly challenges the assumption that a longer delay yields a more robust response by testing a clinically significant earlier schedule in a head-to-head trial.

proposals.title: A Unified Risk Stratification Model to Personalize COVID-19 Vaccination Timing and Antibiotic Stewardship for Secondary Bacterial Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Secondary bacterial pneumonia, particularly involving S. aureus and S. pneumoniae, is a major driver of severe outcomes and mortality in COVID-19. While early vaccination reduces COVID-19 severity, its impact on subsequent bacterial complications is heterogeneous. Simultaneously, antibiotic overuse in COVID-19 patients fuels resistance, necessitating stewardship. A unified framework to identify patients who uniquely benefit from ultra-early vaccination and those at low risk for bacterial complications is critically needed to optimize two key clinical decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients who derive the greatest clinical benefit from very early COVID-19 vaccination (e.g., within 14 days of symptom onset or exposure) are a distinct immunophenotypic subgroup that is concurrently at intrinsically lowest risk for developing severe secondary bacterial pneumonia, enabling a single stratification to guide both accelerated prevention and therapeutic restraint.
proposals.method: We will analyze linked, retrospective electronic health record and vaccination registry data from 2020-2024. The primary outcome is a composite of ICU admission or death with bacterial pneumonia (confirmed by culture or ICD codes) post-COVID-19 diagnosis. We will train a gradient-boosted machine (XGBoost) model using pre-infection host factors (e.g., age, comorbidities, prior vaccination history, lymphocyte count) and precise vaccination timing. The model will predict two linked probabilities: 1) the treatment effect of early vaccination on the primary outcome, and 2) the baseline risk of the outcome without early vaccination. Model interpretability will be assessed using SHAP values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable, data-driven tool to identify high-priority candidates for accelerated vaccination campaigns. It will simultaneously define a low-risk subgroup in whom empirical antibiotic use for suspected bacterial pneumonia could be safely minimized, directly supporting antimicrobial stewardship efforts. The model moves beyond one-size-fits-all guidelines by linking personalized prevention strategy to personalized therapeutic risk.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its explicit conceptual fusion of vaccination optimization and antibiotic stewardship, which are typically siloed interventions. It tests the non-obvious hypothesis that the population most responsive to a viral preventive is biologically the least susceptible to a subsequent bacterial complication, creating a synergistic clinical decision rule.

proposals.title: A Confounder-Aware, Multi-Cohort Study to Determine the Efficacy of Immunologically-Timed Vaccination Delays in High-Risk Patients with Prior S. aureus or COVID-19 Infection
proposals.motivation: Standard vaccination schedules may not be optimal for all patients, particularly those with recent infections or immune dysregulation, as seen in post-MRSA or post-COVID-19 states. Observational studies suggesting benefit from delayed vaccination are confounded by factors like the 'healthy vaccinee' effect, where delay is often due to illness, creating spurious protective associations. A rigorous framework is needed to disentangle true immunological readiness from these confounders to provide personalized guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific, immunologically-defined patient subgroups (e.g., those with recent severe infection or active inflammation), a structured, short-term vaccination delay based on biomarker thresholds will lead to superior humoral and cellular vaccine responses compared to the standard schedule, without increasing the risk of interim infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-arm prospective cohort study in adults with a history of MRSA bacteremia or severe COVID-19. Arms: 1) Standard schedule, 2) Medically-delayed (delay until pre-specified thresholds in platelet count, CRP, and S. aureus-specific T-cell activation are met), 3) Administrative-delayed control. High-frequency immune monitoring will assess vaccine-specific antibody titers, neutralization, and T-cell responses. We will employ targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) and multi-state survival models, integrating real-time local MRSA and SARS-CoV-2 variant circulation data as time-varying covariates, to estimate the stratum-specific effect of delay on immunogenicity and breakthrough infection risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, confounder-aware analytical framework for evaluating non-standard vaccination timing. It will generate the first prospective evidence on whether immunologically-guided delays can improve vaccine response in high-risk, infection-prone populations, directly informing clinical practice for patients similar to those in Dr. Haste's prior work on post-transplant vaccination and antivirulence strategies.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond observational correction by prospectively defining and validating an 'immunological readiness' state for vaccination, integrating pathogen-specific cellular immunity (e.g., S. aureus) with systemic inflammation markers, and explicitly modeling community pathogen pressure as a dynamic modifier of delay benefit.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Host-Response-Guided Delayed Vaccination and Antibiotic Restriction in Infancy: Impact on Immune Development, Microbiome, and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The early infancy period is characterized by frequent empiric medical interventions, including routine vaccination and antibiotic use for suspected infections. Pre-2026 evidence suggests these interventions can disrupt nascent immune system calibration and gut microbiome development, potentially contributing to the rising prevalence of immune-mediated and metabolic disorders. However, blanket delays or restrictions are unsafe; a precision medicine approach is needed to identify infants who can safely defer intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Guiding the timing of routine vaccination and the use of empiric antibiotics via rapid host-response diagnostics will allow for more robust natural immune development and reduce microbiome disruption, leading to a lower incidence of immune dysregulation (e.g., eczema, wheeze) and improved metabolic markers by age 2 years, without increasing serious infection risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized controlled trial in 400 healthy term infants. The intervention arm will use a validated, rapid (<2hr) whole-blood RNA-seq-based host-response panel (leveraging pre-2026 signatures like the Bacterial/Viral Metascore) to guide care: 1) delaying the first routine pneumococcal conjugate vaccine dose until a 'mature' immune transcriptomic phenotype is confirmed, and 2) withholding empiric antibiotics for febrile illness unless a severe bacterial host signature is present. The control arm receives standard-of-care. Primary outcomes are the diversity of the gut microbiome (16S rRNA sequencing) and vaccine-specific antibody titers at 12 months. Secondary outcomes include incidence of clinician-diagnosed eczema, wheeze, and antibiotic-resistant organism colonization (e.g., MRSA) by age 2.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, mechanistic evidence for a synergistic, diagnostic-guided paradigm of 'protective neglect' in infant care. It will generate critical data on the causal links between early interventions, immune-microbiome trajectories, and clinical health outcomes, informing future guidelines for personalized pediatric preventive care.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the default empiric intervention paradigm by using the same host-response diagnostic to guide two distinct, synergistic decisions (vaccination timing and antibiotic use), moving from a calendar-based to a physiology-based schedule of early-life medical care.

proposals.title: A Randomized Non-Inferiority Trial of Shortened (3-Month) vs. Standard (6-Month) SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine Booster Intervals: Immunogenicity, Durability, and Cross-Reactivity Against Variants
proposals.motivation: Optimizing COVID-19 vaccine booster schedules is critical for long-term pandemic control and preparedness. While extended intervals can enhance immunogenicity for primary series, the ideal timing for boosters to maximize durable, cross-protective immunity—especially in vulnerable populations and against emerging variants—remains undefined. This knowledge gap hinders efficient public health policy and individual clinical guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: A three-month booster interval will be non-inferior to a six-month interval in inducing durable, high-quality humoral and cellular immune responses at 12 months post-booster, while potentially offering earlier protection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Adults eligible for a monovalent (e.g., XBB.1.5) mRNA booster will be randomized 1:1 to receive it at 3 or 6 months post-last dose/infection. The primary endpoint is a composite of neutralizing antibody titers against contemporary strains at 12 months post-booster. Secondary/exploratory analyses will use high-parameter flow cytometry for T-cell phenotypes, multiplexed IgG avidity assays, and pseudovirus neutralization against a panel of variants (e.g., BA.2.86, JN.1). Pre-specified subgroup analyses will include age, prior infection, and immunocompromised status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to inform national and global recommendations on optimal booster timing. It will deliver a comprehensive, longitudinal dataset on the quality and breadth of the immune response generated by different intervals, directly addressing a key operational question in vaccinology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on primary series intervals, this is a head-to-head, prospectively randomized comparison of booster intervals explicitly designed to assess long-term durability and cross-reactivity, powered for key immunological secondary endpoints beyond simple titers.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of Serologic Correlates of Protection Against Pneumococcal Disease in High-Risk Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: In immunocompromised populations like allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, pneumococcal vaccination is critical, but vaccine immunogenicity studies often rely on serologic surrogates (e.g., antibody titers) as endpoints. The correlation between these surrogate markers and actual clinical protection against invasive pneumococcal disease (IPD) in real-world, high-risk settings remains inadequately validated, creating uncertainty in clinical guidance. This gap mirrors the need in engineering to validate laboratory material properties under real-world stress conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: In HSCT recipients, a defined post-vaccination serologic response (e.g., a ≥4-fold increase in serotype-specific IgG or an opsonophagocytic assay titer ≥1:8) is directly correlated with a significantly reduced incidence of microbiologically confirmed pneumococcal disease in the subsequent high-risk period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective cohort study of adult allogeneic HSCT recipients vaccinated with the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) followed by the 23-valent polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23), per standard schedules. Serum for serotype-specific IgG and functional (OPA) assays will be drawn pre-vaccination and 4-8 weeks post-vaccination. Participants will then be actively monitored for 24 months via electronic health record review and patient diaries for the primary endpoint: incidence of microbiologically confirmed pneumococcal pneumonia or bacteremia. Statistical analysis will employ Cox proportional hazards models to assess the correlation between achieving the immunologic response threshold and time-to-event, adjusting for covariates like graft-versus-host disease and lymphocyte count.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence directly linking a quantifiable immunologic response to clinical protection against pneumococcal disease in a profoundly immunocompromised cohort. It will inform the clinical utility of post-vaccination serologic testing and strengthen the evidence base for vaccination guidelines in transplant medicine. The findings could also refine the definition of a true 'correlate of protection' for future vaccine trials in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.novelty: While immunogenicity studies in HSCT recipients are common, prospective, active-surveillance studies designed explicitly to validate a serologic correlate against hard clinical endpoints of pneumococcal disease are lacking. This work moves beyond surrogate endpoints to directly test the clinical predictive value of laboratory-based immune responses.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of Graft Source and Conditioning Intensity on Time-to-Seroconversion After Pneumococcal Vaccination in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients: A Targeted Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (allo-HCT) recipients exhibit highly variable and often impaired vaccine responses, leaving them vulnerable to life-threatening infections like those caused by S. pneumoniae. Pre-2026 associative studies, including Dr. Haste's prior work, identify factors like graft source, but cannot disentangle direct causal effects from confounding by time-varying post-transplant factors like immunosuppression and graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). A causal understanding is critical for designing personalized post-HCT vaccination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that graft source (matched unrelated donor vs. haploidentical) and reduced-intensity conditioning have direct, distinct causal effects on the time-to-seroconversion following a standardized pneumococcal vaccine series, even after rigorous adjustment for dynamic post-transplant clinical states.
proposals.method: We will apply a longitudinal causal inference framework using Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE). The statistical model will specify graft source and conditioning intensity as exposures, time-to-seroconversion (anti-pneumococcal IgG ≥0.35 µg/mL) as the outcome, and a pre-specified set of time-varying confounders (e.g., calcineurin inhibitor levels, systemic corticosteroid dose, incident GVHD grade) measured at protocol-defined intervals prior to each vaccine dose. We will use Super Learner, an ensemble machine learning algorithm, to flexibly estimate the exposure and outcome models, enhancing robustness to model misspecification. Sensitivity analyses will employ E-values to quantify the strength of unmeasured confounding required to explain away observed effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, adjusted estimates of the direct causal effects of key transplant characteristics on vaccine response kinetics. It will move the field beyond associative subgroup comparisons, offering evidence to guide clinical decisions on vaccine timing and adjuvant use based on a patient's transplant profile. The application of TMLE in this setting establishes a rigorous analytical framework for future studies of post-HCT immune reconstitution.
proposals.novelty: While prior work describes associations, this is the first to formally apply a longitudinal causal model (TMLE) to estimate the direct effect of transplant characteristics on vaccine response, explicitly adjusting for the dynamic confounding introduced by post-transplant immunosuppression and complications.

proposals.title: Impact of Graft Source and Conditioning Intensity on the Kinetics of Post-Transplant Pneumococcal and SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine Responses in Allogeneic HSCT Recipients
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients are profoundly immunocompromised and at high risk for severe infections, including from Streptococcus pneumoniae and SARS-CoV-2. While vaccination is critical, the timing and magnitude of immune reconstitution are highly variable. Pre-2026 clinical guidelines for post-HSCT vaccination are largely based on fixed timelines, but emerging evidence suggests patient- and transplant-specific factors may critically influence optimal vaccination windows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that graft source (peripheral blood vs. bone marrow vs. cord blood) and conditioning regimen intensity (myeloablative vs. reduced-intensity) are key determinants of the speed and magnitude of B-cell and vaccine-specific antibody reconstitution following allogeneic HSCT.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult allogeneic HSCT recipients (2019-2025) who received pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13/PCV15/PCV20) and/or SARS-CoV-2 mRNA vaccines post-transplant. Using serial, clinically obtained antibody titers (anti-pneumococcal serotype IgG and anti-SARS-CoV-2 spike IgG), we will model response kinetics. We will employ multivariable mixed-effects regression models, with graft source and conditioning intensity as primary exposures, adjusting for age, GVHD, immunosuppression, and time post-transplant. Time-to-seroconversion and area-under-the-curve of antibody titers over the first 24 months will be primary outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide granular, data-driven evidence to refine post-HSCT vaccination schedules, moving beyond one-size-fits-all timelines. It will identify patient subgroups who may benefit from earlier or more intensive vaccination strategies, potentially improving protection against life-threatening infections. The findings will directly inform clinical practice guidelines for immunization in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described vaccine responses at fixed time points, this study uniquely focuses on the *kinetics* of antibody reconstitution and directly tests the influence of fundamental transplant characteristics—graft source and conditioning—which have been underexplored as modulators of vaccine response timing.

proposals.title: A Staged, Biomarker-Driven Vaccine Schedule for Post-HCT Patients: An Ecological Succession Framework for Immune Reconstitution
proposals.motivation: Hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients face high mortality from vaccine-preventable infections. Current guidelines recommend a uniform vaccine schedule starting at 3-6 months post-HCT, but this fails to account for the heterogeneity of immune reconstitution, leading to suboptimal vaccine responses as evidenced by variable pneumococcal vaccine efficacy. A personalized approach is urgently needed to improve protection against pathogens like S. pneumoniae, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling post-HCT immune reconstitution as an ecological succession process, we hypothesize that distinct, measurable immunological stages can be defined, and that specific biomarker thresholds within these stages will predict optimal, pathogen-specific timing for vaccination, leading to superior humoral and cellular immune responses compared to standard schedules.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 150 allogeneic HCT patients, collecting serial blood samples pre- and post-transplant. Using high-dimensional flow cytometry (e.g., spectral cytometry) and multiplex serology, we will quantify immune cell subsets (naive/memory B/T cells, Tregs, NK cells), serum immunoglobulins, and pathogen-specific antibody titers. We will apply unsupervised machine learning (Gaussian Mixture Models, hierarchical clustering) to identify discrete immune reconstitution stages. Subsequently, supervised learning (Random Forest, Cox proportional hazards models) will identify biomarker combinations that predict successful vaccine response (defined as protective titer) to pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID-19 vaccines administered at varying time points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for staging immune recovery post-HCT, moving the field from a time-based to a biomarker-based vaccination paradigm. It will deliver a clinically actionable algorithm to personalize vaccine timing, potentially reducing infection-related morbidity and mortality in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual novelty lies in directly applying ecological succession theory to human immunology, treating immune recovery as a structured, stage-dependent process. Methodologically, it integrates high-dimensional immunophenotyping with machine learning to derive predictive rules for intervention timing, a significant advance over correlative studies.

proposals.title: A Biomarker-Guided Vaccination Strategy for Allogeneic HCT Recipients: Correlating Antigen-Specific Cellular Immune Reconstitution with Vaccine Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients face high mortality from vaccine-preventable infections, including bacterial pathogens like S. pneumoniae and viral threats like COVID-19. Current post-HCT vaccination schedules are based on fixed time intervals, which ignore the significant inter-patient variability in immune reconstitution, leading to suboptimal and heterogeneous responses. A precision approach is urgently needed to improve protection, especially in the context of emerging variants and antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robust, antigen-specific T-follicular helper (Tfh) cell and memory B-cell reconstitution, measured prior to vaccination, is a critical determinant of successful humoral and cellular vaccine responses, and that identifying these milestones will allow for a personalized vaccination schedule superior to calendar-based guidelines.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 120 allogeneic HCT recipients. Using high-parameter flow cytometry (e.g., 28-color panels), we will quantify antigen-specific CD4+ T-cell subsets (including Tfh, Th1, Th17, and memory populations) and memory B-cells at months 3, 6, 9, and 12 post-HCT, in response to vaccine antigens (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 spike, pneumococcal polysaccharides). Antibody titers will be measured via multiplex immunoassays. We will apply machine learning (regularized regression and random forest models) to identify cellular immune signatures predictive of seroconversion and correlate these with clinical infection outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive map of antigen-specific cellular immune reconstitution necessary for effective vaccination post-HCT. It will deliver a validated, biomarker-based algorithm to determine optimal timing for key vaccines, moving the field towards personalized immunology. The findings could directly inform updated clinical guidelines to reduce infection-related morbidity and mortality in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (including the researcher's own) has assessed vaccine response at fixed times, this study proactively defines the requisite immune landscape *before* vaccination by integrating deep cellular phenotyping with machine learning to generate a predictive, rather than reactive, vaccination model.

proposals.title: Optimizing Post-Transplant Immunization: A Prospective, Controlled Study of the Three-Month Vaccination Schedule for Tetanus, Diphtheria, and Pertussis (Tdap) in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients require re-vaccination due to loss of protective immunity. While the three-month post-HCT schedule is established for pneumococcal and influenza vaccines, its efficacy for other critical vaccines like Tdap is not well-defined. Determining the optimal timing for Tdap is crucial to protect this vulnerable population from preventable, life-threatening bacterial infections, especially given their complex immunologic recovery and heightened infection risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Initiating the Tdap vaccine series at three months post-HCT will elicit superior humoral and cellular immune responses compared to later initiation (e.g., six months), without increasing the risk of adverse events or graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) exacerbation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized controlled trial. Adult allogeneic HCT recipients will be randomized 1:1 to initiate a three-dose Tdap series at either 3 months or 6 months post-transplant. The primary endpoint will be the proportion of patients achieving protective anti-pertussis toxin (PT) IgG titers (≥50 IU/mL) one month after the series completion. Secondary endpoints include functional antibody titers against all vaccine antigens (via multiplex immunoassay), pertussis-specific T-cell responses (by ELISpot), rates of GVHD flare, and vaccine safety. Statistical analysis will use intention-to-treat with chi-square and t-tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, controlled evidence for the immunogenicity and safety of the three-month Tdap schedule, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will expand the evidence base for the 'three-month rule' beyond pneumococcal vaccines, potentially streamlining the complex post-HCT vaccination calendar and improving protection against pertussis.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work evaluated pneumococcal vaccine timing, this proposal systematically tests the generalizability of the early vaccination paradigm to a key bacterial vaccine (Tdap) with distinct immunology, addressing a specific evidence gap in current guidelines.

proposals.title: Cellular Immune Correlates of Protection Against Staphylococcus aureus in High-Risk Patients Following Pneumococcal Vaccination
proposals.motivation: Antibody titers alone are an incomplete measure of vaccine efficacy, particularly in immunocompromised hosts like hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, who are at high risk for bacterial infections including S. aureus and pneumococcus. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical role of T-cell and memory B-cell responses in durable protection, especially against variants. This study addresses a key gap in understanding the cellular immune landscape post-vaccination in vulnerable populations prone to S. aureus and respiratory infections.
proposals.hypothesis: In HSCT recipients, the magnitude and quality of antigen-specific T-cell and memory B-cell responses following pneumococcal conjugate vaccination (PCV13) will correlate more strongly with protection against S. aureus colonization and respiratory infection than serum antibody titers alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of allogeneic HSCT recipients receiving standard-of-care PCV13 vaccination. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) will be collected pre-vaccination and at 1 and 6 months post-vaccination. Antigen-specific CD4+ T-cell responses (Th1, Th17, Tfh) to pneumococcal and S. aureus antigens (e.g., clumping factor A) will be quantified via intracellular cytokine staining and flow cytometry. Memory B-cell (MBC) frequencies and their antigen receptor repertoires will be assessed using fluorescently labeled antigen probes and single-cell BCR sequencing. Clinical outcomes (S. aureus/MRSA colonization, respiratory infections) will be tracked for 12 months.
proposals.contribution: This work will define cellular immune correlates of protection against bacterial pathogens in immunocompromised hosts, moving beyond serology. It will provide a mechanistic framework for evaluating vaccine responses in high-risk patients and could identify novel biomarkers for predicting infection risk. The findings may inform the development of adjunctive immunotherapies or improved vaccination strategies targeting S. aureus.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work assessed antibody responses post-vaccination, this proposal integrates deep cellular immunophenotyping with clinical outcomes for S. aureus, directly linking vaccine-induced T/B cell memory to protection against a pathogen not directly targeted by the vaccine (PCV13), suggesting cross-protective mechanisms.

proposals.title: The Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine as a Keystone Intervention to Accelerate Functional Immune Reconstitution and Reduce Infection Risk After Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) recipients suffer from prolonged, profound immune deficiency, leading to high rates of life-threatening infections, including from pathogens like S. aureus and S. pneumoniae. Current strategies for immune reconstitution are largely supportive and passive. An active intervention to catalyze and scale immune recovery would be transformative for patient outcomes, particularly in the context of heightened infection risks post-COVID-19.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that administering the 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) early post-transplant acts as a 'keystone immunological intervention,' accelerating the pace and improving the functional quality of broad immune reconstitution beyond just pneumococcus-specific responses, thereby reducing overall infection burden.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized controlled trial in adult allo-HSCT recipients. Patients will be randomized to receive PCV13 at day +60 or at the standard timepoint (day +180). We will perform serial, high-dimensional immune profiling using mass cytometry (CyTOF) and Olink proteomics pre- and post-vaccination to quantify changes in T-cell (including bystander activation markers) and innate immune cell subsets, and inflammatory mediators. Clinical outcomes (bacterial infections, including MRSA, and overall survival) will be tracked for one year. Statistical analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models and Cox regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence that a specific vaccine can function as a system-wide immune reconstitution catalyst. It will define a novel, active therapeutic strategy to improve post-transplant immunity and directly test the ecological 'keystone' analogy in a clinical immunology context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reconceptualizing vaccination not merely as prophylaxis for a specific pathogen, but as a deliberate, timed immunomodulatory therapy designed to structurally enhance the recovering immune ecosystem, a paradigm shift from existing passive supportive care.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic PK/PD-Immune Model to Optimize Post-Transplant Vaccination Schedules for Global Immune Reconstitution and Protection Against SARS-CoV-2 and S. aureus
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients experience profound and prolonged immunodeficiency, leaving them vulnerable to infections like SARS-CoV-2 and opportunistic pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus. Current vaccination strategies in this population are largely empirical, with poor and variable immunogenicity. There is a critical need to move beyond measuring single-antigen antibody titers and instead develop strategies that accelerate the restoration of broad, functional immunity to prevent life-threatening infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mechanistic mathematical model integrating the pharmacokinetics of vaccine components with the pharmacodynamics of antigen-specific and systemic immune cell expansion can identify personalized vaccination schedules that optimize the rate of global immune reconstitution and clinical protection in transplant recipients.
proposals.method: We will develop a quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model structured as a system of ordinary differential equations. The model will incorporate compartments for vaccine PK (e.g., mRNA/adjuvant kinetics), PD of antigen-specific B and T cell clonal expansion, and the dynamic interplay with recovering innate (e.g., monocytes, NK cells) and adaptive (total CD4+, CD8+ T cells, B cells) immune pools. The model will be calibrated and validated using longitudinal clinical data from HSCT recipients, including serial measurements of immune cell subsets, anti-Spike IgG, and functional assays, alongside infection outcomes. Model simulations will employ optimal control theory to test different vaccination timing and dosing regimens.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive, mechanistic framework to rationally schedule post-transplant vaccinations. It will shift the paradigm from reactive immunogenicity testing to proactive, simulation-driven personalization of immune therapy. The model will serve as a platform to evaluate strategies against multiple pathogens, directly addressing the clinical vulnerabilities highlighted by the researcher's prior work on MRSA and COVID-19 in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of classic antimicrobial PK/PD modeling principles—typically applied to drugs—with the dynamics of vaccine-driven immune reconstitution, creating an integrated 'PK/PD-Immune' model focused on restoring systemic host defense rather than just inducing a single antibody response.

proposals.title: Synergistic Potentiation of Anthracimycin with Standard-of-Care Antibiotics Against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) in Biofilm and Systemic Infection Models
proposals.motivation: The rise of multi-drug resistant pathogens like MRSA, particularly in the context of healthcare-associated infections and secondary bacterial infections post-COVID-19, necessitates novel therapeutic strategies. Anthracimycin, a marine-derived macrolide with potent Gram-positive activity, has shown promise but faces challenges typical of single-agent development, including potential resistance emergence and suboptimal pharmacokinetics. Pre-2026, combination therapies are a recognized strategy to enhance efficacy and delay resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Anthracimycin, developed from the outset as a combination agent, will exhibit synergistic or additive effects with established anti-MRSA antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid), leading to enhanced bacterial killing, biofilm eradication, and suppression of resistance in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.method: We will employ standardized checkerboard broth microdilution assays to determine Fractional Inhibitory Concentration (FIC) indices for anthracimycin paired with 3-4 standard-of-care agents against a panel of clinical MRSA isolates, including USA300. Synergy will be validated using time-kill kinetics. Biofilm eradication will be assessed using crystal violet and confocal microscopy on established biofilms. Promising combinations will be tested in a murine systemic infection model, with bacterial burden in organs (e.g., kidneys, spleen) and survival as primary endpoints. Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling will guide dosing regimens.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the optimal antibiotic partner(s) for anthracimycin, providing a robust preclinical rationale for bypassing single-agent development. It will generate critical data on anthracimycin's utility in combating resilient MRSA biofilms and preventing resistance. The project will deliver a novel, immediately translatable combination therapy blueprint for a pressing antimicrobial resistance threat.
proposals.novelty: While anthracimycin's standalone activity is known, a systematic, de novo development strategy exclusively as a combination agent is novel. This approach directly integrates PK/PD and resistance suppression into the lead optimization phase, contrasting with the traditional paradigm of testing combinations only after single-agent failure.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Machine Learning Classifier for Probabilistic Etiologic Diagnosis of Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: The syndromic diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) remains a major challenge for antibiotic stewardship. The binary culture-based paradigm fails to distinguish viral, typical bacterial (e.g., S. pneumoniae, S. aureus), and atypical pathogens, leading to empirical broad-spectrum antibiotic overuse. This is especially critical in the post-COVID-19 era, where viral-bacterial co-infections and antimicrobial resistance (including MRSA) complicate management.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating clinical data, quantitative chest imaging features, and a panel of host-response biomarkers into a probabilistic machine learning model can accurately classify the etiology of CAP into clinically relevant categories, outperforming standard diagnostic approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-modal classifier using a gradient boosting framework (XGBoost). Inputs will include clinical variables (symptoms, comorbidities), radiomic features extracted from chest X-rays via a pre-trained convolutional neural network (e.g., ResNet50), and serum biomarker levels (e.g., procalcitonin, C-reactive protein, IP-10). The model will be trained on a prospective cohort of CAP patients using an expanded reference standard combining multiplex PCR (BioFire® Pneumonia plus), urinary antigen tests, and paired serology to define etiologic categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, clinically actionable tool that moves CAP diagnosis from syndromic to probabilistic etiologic likelihood, directly informing targeted therapy. It will advance antibiotic stewardship by reducing unnecessary antibacterial use in viral cases and prompt appropriate coverage for bacterial pathogens like S. aureus. The framework establishes a new paradigm for integrating multi-modal data in infectious disease diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior severity scores or single biomarker studies, this approach probabilistically synthesizes three distinct data modalities (clinical, imaging, host-response) specifically for etiology. It moves beyond binary bacterial detection to a multi-class, etiology-focused output trained against a comprehensive molecular and serologic reference standard.

proposals.title: Defining Pathogen-Agnostic Immune Permissiveness Signatures for Bacterial Pneumonia via Integrated Clinical Criteria and Single-Cell Transcriptomics
proposals.motivation: Diagnosing bacterial pneumonia, especially in the context of post-viral (e.g., COVID-19) or post-transplant settings, remains challenging due to the poor sensitivity of microbiological cultures. This leads to syndromic over-treatment with antibiotics, driving resistance in pathogens like MRSA and S. aureus. A culture-independent clinical diagnostic protocol exists, but its biological validity and the specific host immune state it captures are poorly defined, limiting its refinement and adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients meeting culture-independent clinical criteria for bacterial pneumonia will exhibit a distinct, conserved host immune signature—characterized by dysfunctional myeloid cells, altered epithelial responses, and depleted T cell states—that can be defined at single-cell resolution and is agnostic to the specific bacterial pathogen.
proposals.method: We will enroll patients with suspected pneumonia, applying a pre-defined culture-independent diagnostic protocol integrating clinical, radiological (CT), and biomarker (e.g., procalcitonin) criteria. From consenting patients, we will collect matched lower respiratory (BAL) and blood samples for parallel single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Computational analysis will involve integrated clustering (Seurat, Scanpy) to identify cell populations, differential expression analysis to define dysregulated pathways, and cell-cell communication inference (CellChat) to map immune-epithelial crosstalk. The resulting signatures will be used to train a classifier to predict the clinical diagnostic status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, mechanistic validation of a culture-independent pneumonia diagnostic, moving the field from syndromic labels to pathophysiology-informed definitions. It will yield a novel, pathogen-agnostic 'immune permissiveness' signature that can be translated into simpler clinical algorithms or biomarker panels, directly addressing antimicrobial stewardship needs highlighted in Dr. Haste's prior work on antivirulence agents and real-world vaccine effectiveness.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell studies of pneumonia exist, none have been explicitly designed to validate and iteratively refine a specific, actionable clinical diagnostic protocol. The fusion of a ready-for-use clinical algorithm with deep immune phenotyping to create a biologically grounded diagnostic standard is a novel translational approach.

proposals.title: A Culture-Independent Diagnostic Framework for Bacterial Pneumonia: Integrating Clinical, Radiomic, and Host-Response Biomarkers in a Post-COVID-19 Cohort
proposals.motivation: Diagnosing bacterial pneumonia, particularly from pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA, remains challenging due to the low sensitivity of sputum and blood cultures, leading to delayed or inappropriate antibiotic therapy. This problem was exacerbated during the COVID-19 pandemic, where distinguishing viral from bacterial co-infection was critical. A diagnostic approach that does not rely solely on positive cultures is urgently needed to improve patient management and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite diagnostic model integrating clinical parameters, quantitative chest CT radiomic features, and a panel of host-response biomarkers (including platelet activation markers) can accurately identify bacterial pneumonia in hospitalized patients with lower respiratory tract infections, even in the absence of a positive microbiological culture.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients hospitalized with pneumonia (2019-2025). Cases will be defined using a composite clinical-radiological-microbiological gold standard. We will extract structured clinical data, quantify inflammatory biomarkers (e.g., CRP, procalcitonin, platelet factor 4), and perform automated radiomic feature extraction from chest CT scans using a pre-trained convolutional neural network (e.g., a 3D ResNet architecture). A machine learning model (e.g., XGBoost) will be trained to classify bacterial vs. non-bacterial pneumonia, with performance validated against the composite standard and compared to culture-based diagnosis alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, culture-independent diagnostic algorithm for bacterial pneumonia, directly addressing a major clinical bottleneck. It will generate new evidence on the utility of radiomics and platelet-related biomarkers in infection diagnostics, with potential to reduce unnecessary antibiotic use and improve outcomes for patients with culture-negative infections.
proposals.novelty: While individual biomarkers or radiomics have been studied, the integration of a platelet activation signature—informed by the researcher's prior work on platelet-bacterial interactions—with deep learning-based radiomic analysis into a unified diagnostic framework for pneumonia is novel. This approach moves beyond syndromic diagnosis to a data-driven, pathophysiology-informed model.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Diagnostic Framework to Differentiate Colonization from Infection in Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-Negative Opportunistic Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostics often fail to distinguish bacterial colonization from active infection, leading to unnecessary antimicrobial use, patient harm, and antimicrobial resistance. This is a critical problem for organisms like Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and environmental Gram-negatives (e.g., Burkholderia cepacia, Rahnella aquatilis), whose detection does not equate to disease. The COVID-19 pandemic has further highlighted the need for precise host-response diagnostics to guide therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating quantitative microbial data, host immune biomarkers, and clinical context into a unified probabilistic model will significantly improve the accuracy of differentiating true infection from colonization compared to microbial detection alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a machine learning framework using a retrospective cohort of patients with positive cultures for S. aureus or select Gram-negatives, where infection status is adjudicated by expert review. Input features will include: 1) Microbial signal (genomic virulence factors via PCR/sequencing, quantitative load), 2) Host-response signal (serum biomarkers: C-reactive protein, procalcitonin, and platelet indices as inflammatory markers), and 3) Contextual burden (clinical metadata: fever, leukocytosis, organ dysfunction). A random forest or gradient boosting model will be trained to output a probability of true infection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven diagnostic framework to reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing. It will advance the field of antimicrobial stewardship by moving beyond pathogen detection to integrated infection phenotyping, directly addressing a key challenge in managing MRSA and healthcare-associated infections.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual analogy to exoplanet detection, requiring multi-modal 'signals' for confirmation, and in its specific integration of platelet indices and virulence genotyping with traditional biomarkers to create a composite host-pathogen interaction profile.

proposals.title: Colonization Dynamics of Rahnella aquatilis and Burkholderia cepacia as Biomarkers for Functional Immune Reconstitution and Personalized Vaccine Timing in Allogeneic HCT Recipients
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients face high risks of infection and suboptimal vaccine responses due to delayed and variable immune reconstitution. Current post-HCT vaccination schedules are uniform, often beginning at 3-6 months, despite significant inter-patient variability in immune recovery. This one-size-fits-all approach may lead to ineffective immunization in some patients, leaving them vulnerable to pathogens like S. aureus, S. pneumoniae, and respiratory viruses, including SARS-CoV-2.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition from colonization to active infection by specific, low-virulence, opportunistic bacteria (e.g., Rahnella aquatilis, Burkholderia cepacia) in the post-HCT period is a clinically actionable biomarker of insufficient functional immune reconstitution, and microbiome-immune profiling can identify an optimal, personalized timepoint for effective vaccination.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 150 allogeneic HCT recipients, performing serial metagenomic sequencing (shotgun) of stool and respiratory samples to quantify colonization dynamics of target bacteria. This will be integrated with deep immune profiling via mass cytometry (CyTOF) and antigen-specific B- and T-cell responses to standard post-HCT vaccines (pneumococcal, COVID-19). We will employ multivariate joint modeling and time-to-event (Cox) regression to correlate microbial states with immune parameters, and will train a Random Forest model to predict vaccine response failure using baseline and early post-HCT microbiome/immune features.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, microbiome-informed framework for stratifying infection risk post-HCT. It will provide the first clinical evidence for using bacterial colonization dynamics as a real-time biomarker to personalize the timing of vaccination, moving beyond static calendar-based schedules. The resulting predictive model could be integrated into clinical decision support to enhance protective immunity.
proposals.novelty: While microbiome composition is known to associate with HCT outcomes, using the dynamic state (colonization vs. infection) of specific, low-virulence bacteria as a sentinel for systemic immune function is a novel concept. This approach directly links a clinically observable microbiological event to the functional capacity of the reconstituting immune system to guide a specific intervention (vaccination).

proposals.title: Target Trial Emulation of Empiric vs. Culture-Guided Antibiotic Therapy on Clinical Failure in Patients with Suspected Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Empiric antibiotic therapy for suspected bloodstream infections aims to cover likely pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus, including MRSA, but risks unnecessary broad-spectrum use. Observational studies comparing empiric coverage strategies are confounded by severity and timing. The COVID-19 pandemic has further complicated infection management and resource use, highlighting the need for rigorous, real-world evidence to guide stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with suspected S. aureus bacteremia, an empiric antibiotic strategy covering the eventual pathogen at the time of culture order, compared to a non-covering strategy, causally reduces the hazard of clinical failure, defined as a composite of death, ICU transfer, or antibiotic escalation.
proposals.method: We will emulate a target trial using EHR data from adult inpatients with a blood culture ordered and eventual growth of S. aureus. The time-zero is culture order. Exposure is empiric antibiotic receipt (within 24h) of an agent to which the isolate is susceptible. We will implement high-dimensional propensity score (hdPS) matching, incorporating diagnosis codes, vital signs, lab values, and prior microbiology to balance measured confounders. The primary analysis will use a Cox proportional hazards model on the matched cohort to estimate the hazard ratio for time-to-clinical failure, with sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a causal, less biased estimate of the real-world effectiveness of empiric antibiotic coverage for S. aureus, directly informing stewardship protocols. It will demonstrate the application of advanced causal inference methods to a critical infectious disease question, bridging Haste's work on S. aureus antivirulence and real-world effectiveness studies.
proposals.novelty: While target trial emulation is emerging in cardiology and oncology, its rigorous application to antibiotic timing and spectrum choice in bacteremia is novel. This study moves beyond traditional regression adjustment by explicitly emulating randomization and using hdPS to control for a vast array of clinical confounders captured in the EHR.

proposals.title: A Precision Medicine Trial Comparing Empiric vs. Pathogen-Directed Antibiotic Strategies in Hospitalized Patients with Suspected S. aureus Infection: A Multi-Dimensional Outcome Analysis
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic has complicated the management of bacterial co-infections, often leading to increased empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic use, which exacerbates antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and adverse ecological effects like C. difficile infection. Pre-2026, there is a critical need to rigorously quantify the trade-offs between the immediate clinical benefits of empiric therapy and its long-term harms, particularly for high-risk pathogens like MRSA. A holistic, data-driven framework to compare these strategies is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with suspected S. aureus infection, a targeted antibiotic strategy initiated after rapid diagnostic confirmation will yield non-inferior clinical recovery rates while significantly reducing adverse ecological outcomes and healthcare resource utilization compared to a standard empiric broad-spectrum strategy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized controlled trial in hospitalized adults with clinical signs of infection and risk factors for S. aureus. Patients will be randomized to empiric therapy (vancomycin/piperacillin-tazobactam) or a targeted strategy (placebo/supportive care until rapid molecular PCR confirms S. aureus, then pathogen-directed therapy). Using dense electronic health record data as 'clinical sensors,' we will apply joint longitudinal-survival models (e.g., multivariate generalized linear mixed models) to compare trajectories of clinical recovery (SOFA score), alongside time-to-event analyses for secondary outcomes: hospital-acquired C. difficile, emergence of AMR in subsequent cultures, and total hospitalization cost.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a high-resolution, multi-dimensional evidence base to inform antibiotic stewardship guidelines, moving beyond simple efficacy to a holistic assessment of benefit and harm. It will directly quantify the ecological and economic costs of empiricism, supporting more precise, sustainable treatment paradigms. The analytical framework will serve as a model for evaluating other antimicrobial interventions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of the 'pre-emptive vs. targeted' comparative framework from precision agriculture to clinical therapeutics, and in its use of comprehensive longitudinal data modeling to simultaneously capture clinical, microbiological, and economic outcomes in a single trial.

proposals.title: Comparative Risk Factor Profiling for Staphylococcus aureus versus Enterobacteriaceae in Community-Acquired and Healthcare-Associated Pneumonia: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pneumonia remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality, with Staphylococcus aureus and Enterobacteriaceae representing major, clinically distinct etiologies. Empirical antibiotic selection is often broad-spectrum, contributing to antimicrobial resistance. Pre-2026, while risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms are described, a direct, comparative analysis of patient-specific factors predisposing to S. aureus versus Enterobacteriaceae pneumonia is lacking, hindering targeted, pathogen-directed empiric therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, multivariate patient profiles—including comorbidities, recent healthcare exposures, and immune status—differentially predispose individuals to pneumonia caused by S. aureus versus Enterobacteriaceae, independent of traditional classifications like healthcare-associated pneumonia (HCAP).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. Adult patients with culture-confirmed S. aureus or Enterobacteriaceae pneumonia will be identified. We will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify independent risk factors for each pathogen. To handle high-dimensional clinical data and identify complex interactions, we will use machine learning algorithms, specifically a Random Forest classifier and LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) regression, for feature selection and predictive model building, comparing their performance to traditional regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a validated clinical risk score to help clinicians estimate the pre-test probability of S. aureus versus Enterobacteriaceae at pneumonia diagnosis, informing more precise initial antibiotic choice. It will also elucidate the distinct pathophysiological niches of these pathogens, contributing to the understanding of host-microbe interactions in lung infection.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond grouping pathogens by resistance pattern to directly compare risk factors for two major Gram-positive and Gram-negative causes of pneumonia. It integrates modern machine learning feature selection with traditional epidemiological methods to uncover novel, non-linear associations within pre-2026 clinical data.

proposals.title: A Culture-Independent Machine Learning Diagnostic for Bacterial Pneumonia in COVID-19 Using Sepsis-Pretrained Models and Host-Response Signatures
proposals.motivation: Diagnosing bacterial co-infection in COVID-19 is a major clinical challenge, as traditional microbiological cultures have low sensitivity in this population, leading to both overuse of antibiotics and missed infections. This evidence gap hampers antimicrobial stewardship efforts and obscures the true epidemiology of pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA in COVID-19 patients. A reliable, culture-independent diagnostic tool is urgently needed to guide therapy and improve patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model, initialized with weights from models pre-trained on early sepsis detection for their sensitivity to systemic infection signals, can accurately diagnose and phenotype bacterial pneumonia in COVID-19 patients using routinely available clinical, laboratory, and biomarker data, without requiring positive microbiological cultures.
proposals.method: We will develop a multimodal deep learning framework, likely using a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer architecture to model temporal lab trends (e.g., platelet count, WBC), combined with convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for chest imaging features extracted from reports. These will be integrated with static host-response biomarkers (e.g., procalcitonin, CRP). The model will be pre-trained on large sepsis detection datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III) and fine-tuned on a retrospective cohort of COVID-19 patients with adjudicated infection status. Specific algorithms like XGBoost will be used for feature selection and model interpretability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, probabilistic diagnostic tool for bacterial pneumonia in COVID-19, enabling more precise patient stratification for antimicrobial stewardship trials. It will also generate novel epidemiological insights into the incidence and clinical phenotypes of bacterial co-infections, particularly involving S. aureus, in the post-COVID-19 era.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely repurposes sepsis-pretrained models for a distinct diagnostic task in pneumonia, leveraging their learned sensitivity to early systemic signals. It moves beyond culture-dependent definitions to create a purely clinical diagnostic framework, integrating temporal data trends with host-response biomarkers in a way not previously applied to this specific clinical question.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Metagenomic Profiling of the Respiratory Resistome Predicts Risk of Vaccine Breakthrough Pneumococcal Disease
proposals.motivation: Despite pneumococcal vaccination, high-risk populations like older adults and immunocompromised patients remain vulnerable to breakthrough disease. Pre-2026 research indicates that vaccine efficacy is variable and that the respiratory microbiome, particularly its reservoir of antimicrobial resistance genes (the resistome), may influence infection risk. A comprehensive, longitudinal understanding of how vaccination interacts with the entire microbial community to shape disease outcomes is critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pre-vaccination composition and longitudinal dynamics of the respiratory microbiome and resistome, characterized by deep metagenomic sequencing, are predictive of pneumococcal vaccine failure and subsequent disease risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of high-risk adults (e.g., allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients) undergoing standard-of-care pneumococcal vaccination. Serial nasopharyngeal/oropharyngeal swabs will be collected pre-vaccination and at defined intervals post-vaccination. Samples will undergo deep shotgun metagenomic sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq). Bioinformatic analysis will use established pre-2026 pipelines: KneadData for quality control, MetaPhlAn for taxonomic profiling, and the Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD) via the Resistance Gene Identifier (RGI) for resistome characterization. Longitudinal microbial and resistome profiles will be statistically correlated with rigorously adjudicated clinical outcomes of pneumococcal disease (pneumonia, bacteremia).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-resolution framework for predicting individual risk of vaccine failure based on microbial ecology. It will identify specific bacterial strains and resistance gene constellations associated with breakthrough disease, moving beyond serotype-based surveillance. The findings could inform personalized vaccination strategies and novel antimicrobial stewardship approaches targeting the resistome.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have cataloged pathogens or resistance genes, this proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal, strain-resolved metagenomics with prospective clinical outcomes to directly test the ecological mediation of vaccine efficacy, a hypothesis not yet rigorously examined in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Bayesian, Host-Response-Informed PK/PD Model for Personalizing Anti-Staphylococcal Therapy in COVID-19-Associated Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Bacterial pneumonia, particularly from Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA, is a frequent and lethal complication of severe COVID-19. Standard antibiotic dosing, derived from general ICU populations, fails in this context due to profound, dynamic pathophysiological changes (e.g., hyperinflammation, capillary leak, organ dysfunction) that unpredictably alter drug pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. This mismatch risks treatment failure and accelerates antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time, patient-specific host-response biomarkers (e.g., IL-6, angiopoietin-2) and bacterial load into a mechanism-based PK/PD model will enable adaptive, individualized antibiotic dosing that achieves target exposure more reliably than standard-of-care regimens in COVID-19 pneumonia patients.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical, mechanism-informed PK/PD model linking drug (e.g., vancomycin) disposition to time-varying covariates (cytokines, endothelial markers, renal function). A Bayesian learning framework, using algorithms like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Stan) for parameter estimation, will assimilate high-frequency clinical data (serum drug levels, biomarkers from biobanked samples, qPCR for S. aureus). This will drive a web-based dashboard providing real-time, posterior predictive dosing recommendations, validated in a retrospective cohort of COVID-19/MRSA pneumonia patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive modeling platform for precision antibiotic dosing in a complex, immunodysregulated host. It will provide the first quantitative framework linking COVID-19-specific host response to antibiotic PK/PD, with the potential to improve clinical outcomes and serve as a blueprint for managing infections in other hyperinflammatory states.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static models, this approach dynamically updates dosing using a mechanism-based model of COVID-19 pathophysiology. It moves beyond drug levels alone by integrating host-response data as direct covariates for PK/PD prediction, a strategy not yet applied to antibacterial therapy in this population.

proposals.title: Mapping the Therapeutic Niche: An Ecological Niche Modeling Approach to Predict Vancomycin Efficacy for MRSA Pneumonia in Critically Ill COVID-19 Patients
proposals.motivation: Secondary bacterial pneumonia, particularly from MRSA, is a major cause of mortality in severe COVID-19 patients. Standard antibiotic dosing, based on population pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models, often fails due to the profound and dynamic pathophysiological alterations in these patients, including hyperinflammation, capillary leak, and organ dysfunction. This leads to subtherapeutic drug exposure and treatment failure, highlighting an urgent need for personalized, physiology-informed dosing strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the complex host physiology of a severe COVID-19 patient defines a 'therapeutic niche' for antibiotic efficacy, and that ecological niche modeling algorithms can integrate multivariate patient data with drug concentrations to predict bacterial kill rates more accurately than traditional PK/PD models alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) species distribution modeling algorithm. Patient 'presence' data will be defined as timepoints where a target pharmacodynamic index (e.g., AUC/MIC) is achieved alongside successful bacterial kill (from serial quantitative cultures). Environmental variables will include dynamic physiological parameters (e.g., IL-6, CRP, fluid balance, renal/hepatic function scores) and vancomycin concentrations. The model will be trained and validated using a longitudinal cohort of COVID-19 patients with MRSA pneumonia, generating a predictive map of the 'niche' where treatment is effective.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, interpretable modeling framework that explicitly links host inflammatory and physiological state to antibiotic pharmacodynamics. It will provide a proof-of-concept for moving beyond static PK/PD to dynamic, patient-specific efficacy predictions, directly addressing the therapeutic challenges in complex, immunodysregulated hosts. The resulting model could form the basis for a clinical decision support tool for real-time dose adjustment.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct conceptual and technical translation of ecological niche modeling to a clinical pharmacology problem, treating the host as a dynamic landscape for drug-bacteria interaction. It integrates the researcher's expertise in antivirulence strategies and real-world COVID-19 outcomes with a unique methodological analogy from conservation biology.

proposals.title: Personalized PK/PD Modeling to Optimize Anti-Staphylococcal Antibiotic Dosing in Critically Ill COVID-19 Patients with Secondary Bacterial Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Critically ill COVID-19 patients, especially those in the ICU, exhibit profound pathophysiological changes (e.g., capillary leak, altered renal/hepatic function, hyperinflammation) that drastically alter antibiotic pharmacokinetics (PK). This leads to a high risk of subtherapeutic dosing and treatment failure for life-threatening secondary bacterial pneumonias, particularly those caused by pathogens like MRSA. Existing standard dosing regimens, derived from non-COVID populations, are inadequate, necessitating a precision medicine approach grounded in real-world patient data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting established ICU population PK/PD models to incorporate COVID-19-specific physiological covariates will generate patient-specific dosing regimens that achieve target pharmacodynamic (PD) exposures (e.g., fT>MIC, AUC/MIC) for anti-staphylococcal antibiotics more reliably than standard of care, thereby improving the probability of treatment success.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of COVID-19 ICU patients with culture-confirmed S. aureus pneumonia treated with vancomycin or linezolid. Using rich, sparse, and scavenged drug concentration-time data, we will develop a population PK model via non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). Key covariates will include COVID-19-specific markers (e.g., interleukin-6 levels, C-reactive protein), fluid balance, and organ dysfunction scores. We will then perform Monte Carlo simulations (MCS) to assess the probability of target attainment (PTA) of standard and model-informed dosing regimens against local MRSA MIC distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first COVID-19-specific PK/PD models for key anti-staphylococcal antibiotics, directly addressing a critical clinical knowledge gap. It will deliver a practical, simulation-based dosing framework that clinicians can use to personalize therapy, aiming to reduce treatment failure and potentially slow antimicrobial resistance by ensuring optimal exposure.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling in ICU patients is established, this approach is novel by explicitly integrating the unique inflammatory and physiological 'fingerprint' of severe COVID-19 as model covariates, moving beyond generic critical illness. It directly builds on Dr. Haste's prior work on antivirulence strategies against S. aureus by addressing the complementary problem of optimizing conventional antibiotic efficacy in this vulnerable cohort.

proposals.title: Validating In Vitro Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) Indices for Antivirulence Compounds Against Staphylococcus aureus in a Humanized Platelet-Based Infection Model
proposals.motivation: The failure of in vitro potency to predict in vivo efficacy is a major bottleneck in anti-infective development, particularly for antivirulence agents like diflunisal against S. aureus. This is exacerbated in complex infection settings, such as COVID-19 co-infections, where host factors like platelets and immune dysregulation alter pathogen behavior.
