Pre-2026, standard PK/PD indices (e.g., AUC/MIC) are validated for traditional antibiotics but not for antivirulence strategies in physiologically relevant environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vitro PK/PD indices derived from dynamic time-kill experiments in a human platelet-containing medium will directly predict in vivo efficacy in a murine model of S. aureus infection, whereas indices from standard bacteriological media will not.
proposals.method: We will first establish in vitro PK/PD for diflunisal and a comparator antibiotic against MRSA using a hollow-fiber infection model (HFIM) with two media: standard Mueller-Hinton broth and a novel medium supplemented with human platelets to mimic vascular infection niches. We will quantify bacterial killing and toxin production (e.g., alpha-toxin) to calculate PK/PD indices (AUC/EC50). These indices will then be used to design dosing regimens for validation in a neutropenic murine thigh infection model. The correlation between in vitro-predicted and in vivo-observed efficacy will be statistically evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, physiologically informed PK/PD framework for preclinical development of antivirulence agents, potentially accelerating their translation. It will also offer mechanistic insight into how host components like platelets modulate drug efficacy, bridging the gap between simple in vitro assays and complex in vivo outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly challenging the translational assumption by using a host-factor-enhanced in vitro system (platelet-supplemented HFIM) to generate PK/PD indices, rather than relying on standard methods, and prospectively testing their predictive power in vivo.

proposals.title: Disentangling Host-Directed Anti-Inflammatory and Direct Antivirulence Effects of Diflunisal in Staphylococcus aureus Infection Models
proposals.motivation: The repurposing of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like diflunisal as antivirulence agents against S. aureus (including MRSA) is a promising therapeutic strategy. However, NSAIDs inherently possess potent host-directed anti-inflammatory effects, which can independently improve infection outcomes by modulating the immune response. This creates a critical confounding factor in preclinical models, where observed benefits may be incorrectly attributed solely to direct bacterial targeting, potentially misguiding clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the protective effect of diflunisal in S. aureus infection models is mediated by its host anti-inflammatory action, separable from its direct inhibition of bacterial virulence factors.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-model approach using established murine models of S. aureus bacteremia and skin/soft tissue infection. The core design involves comparing wild-type C57BL/6 mice with mice deficient in key prostaglandin E2 receptors (EP2/EP4), the primary mediators of NSAID anti-inflammatory effects. Both groups will be treated with diflunisal or vehicle control. Primary outcomes will be bacterial burden (CFU) and host cytokine profiles (via Luminex multiplex assays). We will complement this with in vitro assays using human platelets and neutrophils to assess diflunisal's direct impact on S. aureus virulence phenotypes (e.g., alpha-toxin production, fibrinogen binding) in the absence of a host immune system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for deconvoluting host- versus pathogen-directed effects of repurposed NSAIDs. It will establish clearer efficacy benchmarks for true antivirulence agents and inform more rational design of combination therapies targeting both the pathogen and dysregulated host inflammation, particularly relevant in the context of post-viral (e.g., COVID-19) bacterial co-infections.
proposals.novelty: While diflunisal's antivirulence activity is known, no study has systematically dissected its anti-inflammatory contribution to in vivo efficacy using receptor-knockout models. This approach directly addresses a fundamental, yet overlooked, confounder in the antivirulence drug discovery pipeline.

proposals.title: Repurposing Diflunisal as a Broad-Spectrum Antivirulence Agent: Targeting AgrA-Homologous Quorum Sensing Regulators in Staphylococci and Streptococci
proposals.motivation: The rise of antibiotic resistance necessitates novel antimicrobial strategies like antivirulence therapy, which disarms pathogens without killing them, reducing selective pressure. Diflunisal, an FDA-approved NSAID, was recently shown to inhibit the Agr quorum sensing system in Staphylococcus aureus, a key regulator of virulence. This presents a compelling opportunity to explore its efficacy against other clinically significant pathogens that utilize homologous response regulators, potentially offering a repurposed, broad-spectrum antivirulence agent.
proposals.hypothesis: Diflunisal will inhibit virulence phenotypes in bacterial species possessing AgrA-homologous response regulators, specifically targeting the conserved ligand-binding domain to disrupt quorum sensing and toxin production.
proposals.method: We will first perform a bioinformatic analysis to identify pathogens with AgrA-homologous regulators (e.g., in Streptococcus pyogenes, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Listeria monocytogenes) based on sequence and structural homology. For top candidates, we will conduct in vitro assays measuring diflunisal's impact on key virulence outputs: hemolysin activity, biofilm formation, and expression of regulator-controlled toxins via qRT-PCR. Dose-response curves will determine IC50 values. We will use isogenic mutant strains lacking the target regulator as controls to confirm specificity. Finally, we will employ molecular docking simulations (using AutoDock Vina) to model diflunisal binding to homology-built structures of the identified AgrA homologs.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the spectrum of diflunisal's antivirulence activity, identifying new target pathogens beyond S. aureus. It will provide mechanistic insight into the conservation of the AgrA-like binding pocket. The findings could directly inform preclinical development of diflunisal or its derivatives as adjunctive therapies for infections caused by multiple Gram-positive pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While diflunisal's anti-Agr activity in S. aureus is known, its potential as a cross-species, broad-spectrum quorum sensing inhibitor has not been systematically explored. This proposal moves beyond a single pathogen to test a novel 'one-drug, multiple-targets' antivirulence strategy based on conserved regulatory homology.

proposals.title: Adjunctive Diflunisal Enhances the Efficacy of Standard-of-Care Antibiotics Against Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) in Preclinical Models
proposals.motivation: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality, with treatment options increasingly limited by resistance. The repurposing of non-antibiotic drugs like diflunisal as antivirulence agents offers a promising strategy to extend the utility of existing antibiotics. This is particularly urgent in the post-COVID-19 era, where secondary bacterial infections, including MRSA, have complicated patient outcomes and strained healthcare resources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that diflunisal, by inhibiting key S. aureus virulence factors (e.g., via modulation of the agr quorum-sensing system), will act synergistically with standard-of-care antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin, daptomycin, linezolid) to improve bacterial killing and clinical outcomes in models of resistant S. aureus infection.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of in vitro and in vivo models. In vitro, checkerboard broth microdilution assays will determine the fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC) indices for diflunisal combined with specific antibiotics against a panel of clinical MRSA isolates. Synergistic combinations will be further evaluated in time-kill assays and using human platelet-bacteria interaction models to assess impact on virulence phenotypes like platelet aggregation. In vivo efficacy will be tested in a murine neutropenic thigh infection model, measuring bacterial burden reduction in animals treated with antibiotic alone versus antibiotic plus diflunisal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical preclinical data on the specific antibiotic partners for which diflunisal shows the greatest adjunctive benefit, directly informing future clinical trial design. It will elucidate whether antivirulence-antibiotic combinations can lower the effective antibiotic dose, potentially reducing toxicity and slowing resistance emergence. The findings will advance the field of antibiotic stewardship by validating a novel, non-traditional combination therapy approach.
proposals.novelty: While diflunisal's standalone antivirulence activity is established, its systematic evaluation as a synergistic adjunct to specific, clinically relevant antibiotics against MRSA is novel. This proposal uniquely bridges the researcher's expertise in antivirulence repurposing (diflunisal) with translational models of infection and antibiotic efficacy, moving beyond single-agent studies.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Source of Protection: A Matched Cohort Analysis of Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection and Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab (Evusheld) in High-Risk Patients During Omicron BA.2/BA.5 Dominance
proposals.motivation: Real-world effectiveness studies of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C) during Omicron subvariant waves have shown reduced protection against hospitalization. Concurrently, high rates of prior infection have conferred significant natural immunity. For immunocompromised patients, distinguishing the source of protection is critical for clinical guidance and resource allocation, as the continued use of a potentially ineffective monoclonal antibody carries cost and logistical burdens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed reduction in COVID-19 hospitalization among patients indicated for T/C prophylaxis during Omicron BA.2/BA.5 dominance is primarily attributable to immunity from prior SARS-CoV-2 infection, not to the administration of T/C itself.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity score-matched cohort study using electronic health record data from a multi-hospital system (Jan 2022 - Dec 2023). The exposed cohort will be T/C recipients. The comparator will be T/C-eligible, untreated patients matched 1:1 on propensity score (including demographics, comorbidities, immunosuppression level, vaccination status, and prior infection status). The primary outcome is COVID-19 hospitalization within 90 days. A key sub-analysis will compare outcomes in matched pairs where both have prior infection, testing for a null effect of T/C. We will use Cox proportional hazards models, stratifying by prior infection status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial evidence on the real-world effectiveness of T/C in the context of widespread prior infection, directly informing clinical practice for immunocompromised patient management. It will advance methodological approaches for isolating drug effects in observational studies of infectious diseases with high background immunity. The findings will have implications for the use of other monoclonal antibodies in evolving pandemic landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies report T/C effectiveness, none have explicitly designed an analysis to test the null hypothesis that its observed benefit is an artifact of confounding by prior infection immunity. This approach directly challenges the assumed mechanism of protection using a matched study design that isolates this specific confounder.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study and Machine Learning Framework for Real-Time Estimation and Forecasting of Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab Effectiveness Against Emerging SARS-CoV-2 Omicron Subvariants
proposals.motivation: The rapid, continuous evolution of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron subvariants, such as BA.2.86 and JN.1, has rendered monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) like tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C) ineffective against many lineages, creating a critical need for agile surveillance. Current vaccine effectiveness (VE) studies are inherently retrospective, lagging behind viral evolution and leaving public health decisions reactive. A framework for near real-time assessment and predictive forecasting of mAb and vaccine effectiveness is urgently required to protect vulnerable populations, including immunocompromised patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a predictive model integrating prospective, linked genomic-clinical data with longitudinal serological neutralization titers against a broad variant panel can accurately forecast the clinical effectiveness of T/C and COVID-19 vaccines against newly detected and phylogenetically projected Omicron subvariants.
proposals.method: We will establish a multi-national, prospective cohort of high-risk patients (e.g., stem cell transplant recipients) with linked viral sequencing and electronic health records to calculate real-world T/C VE against currently circulating subvariants using a test-negative case-control design. Concurrently, we will train a gradient boosting machine (XGBoost) model using features from three data streams: (1) aggregated VE estimates from our cohort, (2) longitudinal pseudovirus neutralization assay data from a human serum panel against a historical and contemporary variant panel, and (3) quantified antigenic distances and phylogenetic growth rates derived from global GISAID sequence data. The model will be validated by its ability to 'predict' the known drop in T/C effectiveness against historical variants like BA.5 and XBB.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective, real-world evidence of T/C effectiveness against the latest Omicron subvariants with minimal latency. It will deliver a validated, operational predictive model that outputs a continuously updated risk score for new variants, transforming mAb and vaccine deployment from a reactive to a proactive strategy. The framework will be a prototype for assessing other therapeutic antibodies and next-generation vaccines.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies reported historical T/C effectiveness, this is the first to integrate prospective clinical surveillance with serological and phylogenetic data into a live forecasting model. The novelty lies in the closed-loop, adversarial design where the model is continuously updated and validated against real-world outcomes, creating a tool for anticipatory public health action rather than retrospective analysis.

proposals.title: Antigenic Cartography of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron Subvariants to Model and Predict Neutralization by Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab
proposals.motivation: The rapid antigenic evolution of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron subvariants has rendered most monoclonal antibody therapies ineffective, creating an urgent need for tools to forecast the clinical utility of remaining agents like Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab (T/C). Current assessments are reactive, lagging behind variant emergence. A proactive, predictive model is required to guide clinical use and public health policy for immunocompromised patients who rely on these therapies for protection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that antigenic cartography, a method successfully used to model influenza virus drift and vaccine strain selection, can accurately map the antigenic evolution of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron sub-lineages and quantitatively predict the in vitro neutralization potency of T/C against future variants based on their genetic and antigenic distance.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput focus reduction neutralization tests (FRNT) using T/C against a panel of 20-25 SARS-CoV-2 variants, from ancestral to contemporary Omicron subvariants (e.g., BA.2, BA.4/5, BQ.1, XBB). Neutralization titers will be used as input for antigenic cartography, implemented via the 'Racmacs' package in R, which uses multidimensional scaling to position viruses and sera in an antigenic map. We will validate the model's predictive power by withholding later variants, using genetic sequence data to infer their map position, and comparing predicted vs. measured neutralization titers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, antigenic map of Omicron evolution relative to a key therapeutic monoclonal antibody. It will deliver a computational tool to estimate T/C effectiveness against newly sequenced variants prior to clinical outcome data, enabling more agile clinical guidance. Furthermore, it will establish a generalizable framework for assessing other monoclonal antibodies or convalescent plasma.
proposals.novelty: While antigenic cartography is established in influenza research, its application to model the antigenic drift of SARS-CoV-2 from the fixed perspective of a monoclonal antibody therapeutic, rather than polyclonal sera, is novel. This adapts a classic virological method to solve a pressing therapeutic forecasting problem in real-time.

proposals.title: Comparative Real-World Effectiveness of Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab Versus Alternative Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis Strategies for COVID-19 in High-Risk Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised patients, such as allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients, often mount suboptimal responses to COVID-19 vaccination and remain at high risk for severe outcomes. While tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C) was authorized for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP), its real-world effectiveness relative to other available options like bebtelovimab or oral antivirals (e.g., nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) used as PrEP in off-label settings is unclear, especially against evolving Omicron subvariants. Direct comparative data is urgently needed to guide clinical decision-making for this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a real-world cohort of high-risk immunocompromised patients, the comparative effectiveness of T/C for preventing COVID-19 hospitalization and mortality will differ significantly from that of alternative prophylactic agents, and that this effectiveness will vary based on the predominant SARS-CoV-2 variant and patient-specific factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter cohort study using linked electronic health record and claims data from the National COVID Cohort Collaborative (N3C) enclave. The cohort will include immunocompromised adults who received T/C, bebtelovimab, or off-label oral antiviral PrEP between December 2021 and December 2023. The primary outcome is a composite of COVID-19 hospitalization or death within 90 days. We will use propensity score matching to balance groups and employ Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, vaccination status, and regional variant prevalence. Subgroup analyses will focus on specific immunocompromising conditions, including post-transplant patients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world comparative effectiveness analysis of available PrEP strategies for COVID-19 in immunocompromised patients, directly addressing a critical evidence gap. The findings will inform clinical guidelines and payer policies on optimal prophylactic selection. Furthermore, the analysis of variant-specific effectiveness will offer insights into the durability of monoclonal antibodies versus antiviral agents.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work evaluated T/C's effectiveness in isolation, this proposal directly compares it against other clinically used prophylactic modalities in a head-to-head design. The integration of temporal variant data and focus on a heterogeneous immunocompromised population, building on the researcher's expertise in post-transplant infection, moves beyond descriptive reports to causal inference in a complex, evolving therapeutic landscape.

proposals.title: Clinical Severity and Viral Kinetics of SARS-CoV-2 Breakthrough Infections in High-Risk Patients Following Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis with Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab (Evusheld)
proposals.motivation: By late 2025, tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C) remains a critical pre-exposure prophylaxis for immunocompromised patients with poor vaccine responses. However, the clinical spectrum of post-T/C breakthrough infections, especially against Omicron subvariants, is not fully characterized beyond hospitalization rates. A detailed analysis of infection severity, symptom duration, and viral dynamics is needed to inform clinical management and future monoclonal antibody development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-T/C breakthrough SARS-CoV-2 infections in high-risk patients will demonstrate attenuated clinical severity and accelerated viral clearance compared to matched, high-risk controls without T/C prophylaxis, despite similar rates of hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter cohort study of immunocompromised adults (e.g., stem cell transplant recipients) with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. The exposed cohort will be patients with prior T/C administration, matched 1:2 to unexposed controls. Primary outcomes will be a composite severity score incorporating symptom burden (PROs), oxygen requirements, and organ-specific complications. Secondary outcomes include time to symptom resolution and longitudinal cycle threshold (Ct) values from routine PCR tests. We will use multivariable ordinal logistic regression for the severity score and mixed-effects models for viral kinetics, adjusting for variant timing, vaccine status, and comorbidities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first granular, multi-dimensional assessment of COVID-19 severity in T/C breakthrough cases, quantifying the 'clinical attenuation' effect. It will inform risk-benefit discussions for prophylaxis use and establish a framework for evaluating the real-world effectiveness of monoclonal antibodies against endpoints more sensitive than hospitalization or death.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established T/C's effectiveness against hospitalization, this study uniquely focuses on the detailed clinical and virological course of breakthrough infections, leveraging patient-reported outcomes and serial viral load data—an analysis not performed in the pivotal trials or initial real-world effectiveness studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Real-World Impact of Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab on Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA Infection Trends in High-Risk Cohorts: An Interrupted Time-Series Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C) was a critical pre-exposure prophylaxis for COVID-19 in immunocompromised patients. However, its impact on the broader infection landscape, particularly on bacterial pathogens like Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA, remains unquantified. Given the known interplay between viral and bacterial infections and the high-risk nature of these patients, understanding whether T/C administration inadvertently altered the epidemiology of serious bacterial infections is a crucial public health question.
proposals.hypothesis: The widespread administration of T/C for COVID-19 prophylaxis in immunocompromised populations caused a statistically significant interruption in the pre-existing time-series trend of S. aureus and MRSA infection rates, likely due to the reduction in SARS-CoV-2 infection as a predisposing risk factor for secondary bacterial infection.
proposals.method: We will apply segmented regression, a core econometric interrupted time-series analysis (ITSA) method, to model weekly or monthly incidence rates of S. aureus/MRSA infections in a defined high-risk cohort (e.g., allogeneic HSCT recipients) from a large healthcare system database. The intervention point will be the date of T/C administration for each patient. The model will estimate pre-intervention level and trend, immediate level change post-T/C, and change in trend post-T/C, using Newey-West standard errors to account for autocorrelation. Sensitivity analyses will include control series of patients not receiving T/C.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of the indirect, population-level effect of a monoclonal antibody against SARS-CoV-2 on subsequent bacterial infection dynamics. It will offer evidence for or against a 'protective cascade' effect of antiviral prophylaxis. The findings will inform infection prevention strategies and highlight the value of time-series methods for evaluating the secondary impacts of therapeutic interventions.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work assessed the direct clinical effectiveness of T/C against COVID-19, this proposal innovatively applies an econometric causal inference method (ITSA) to model its indirect effect on unrelated bacterial pathogens, creating a novel link between virology, bacteriology, and health econometrics.

proposals.title: Correlation of Individual Post-Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab Antibody Titers and T-Cell Immunity with SARS-CoV-2 Breakthrough Infection Risk in High-Risk Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: The monoclonal antibody combination tixagevimab/cilgavimab (T/C, Evusheld) was a critical pre-exposure prophylaxis for immunocompromised patients with poor vaccine responses during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, real-world effectiveness studies in the Omicron era showed breakthrough infections occurred, with protection likely varying based on individual immune status. Defining a precise, patient-level correlate of protection is essential for rational clinical decision-making and future prophylactic development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific post-T/C administration antibody titers (anti-Spike IgG) and functional cellular immunity markers (SARS-CoV-2-specific IFN-γ release) will be inversely correlated with the risk and severity of SARS-CoV-2 breakthrough infection in a high-risk cohort.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) recipients who received T/C. Serial blood samples will be collected post-administration. Anti-Spike IgG titers will be quantified via ELISA, and SARS-CoV-2-specific T-cell responses will be measured via interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA). Breakthrough infections will be actively monitored via PCR and clinical records. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, with time-dependent antibody and cellular immunity covariates, to model breakthrough infection risk, adjusting for factors like lymphopenia and time post-transplant.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish patient-level immunological correlates of protection for T/C, moving beyond population-level effectiveness. It will provide clinicians with quantitative benchmarks to identify patients who remain at high risk despite prophylaxis, guiding the need for additional protective measures. The findings will also inform the design of future monoclonal antibody and antiviral prophylaxis strategies.
proposals.novelty: While population-level T/C effectiveness is known, this study uniquely integrates serial quantitative antibody AND functional cellular immunity data to model individual breakthrough risk in a profoundly immunocompromised cohort, a group underrepresented in prior correlate of protection studies.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Preemptive Antifungal Therapy Based Solely on Serum Galactomannan Positivity in High-Risk Hematology Patients
proposals.motivation: Invasive aspergillosis (IA) is a leading cause of mortality in immunocompromised patients, such as allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients. The serum galactomannan (GM) assay is a key biomarker for IA, but its use typically triggers confirmatory diagnostics (e.g., CT imaging, bronchoalveolar lavage), delaying therapy. A strategy of initiating therapy based on GM positivity alone could expedite treatment but risks overtreatment and toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: In high-risk hematology patients, a clinical pathway triggering preemptive antifungal therapy based solely on a single positive serum GM index (≥0.7) will be non-inferior in terms of 30-day all-cause mortality compared to a standard diagnostic-driven pathway, and will reduce the time to antifungal initiation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, single-center cohort study of adult hematology patients with a positive serum GM assay (Platelia™ Aspergillus Ag) from 2020-2024. Patients will be stratified into two groups: those who received preemptive antifungals within 48 hours of the first positive GM without confirmatory workup (Intervention), and those whose therapy was guided by subsequent diagnostic evaluation (Control). Primary outcomes are 30-day mortality and time to therapy. We will use propensity score matching based on neutropenia duration, corticosteroid use, and underlying malignancy to control for confounding. Secondary analyses will assess rates of proven/probable IA, antifungal-related adverse events, and hospital length of stay.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide real-world evidence on the safety and efficacy of a streamlined, biomarker-driven antifungal initiation strategy. It will inform clinical guideline development by quantifying the trade-offs between early intervention and diagnostic stewardship in a vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While GM-guided preemptive therapy is discussed, existing protocols universally incorporate confirmatory testing. This study directly evaluates the outcomes of deliberately bypassing these steps, a clinically significant but under-researched de-escalation of diagnostic complexity.

proposals.title: Extended Surveillance for Invasive Mold Infections in Allogeneic HSCT Recipients with Chronic GVHD: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Redefine the High-Risk Period
proposals.motivation: The 100-day post-transplant period is a well-established high-risk window for invasive fungal infections (IFIs) in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients. However, patients with chronic graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), requiring prolonged immunosuppression, remain at significant risk for late-onset IFIs, particularly invasive mold infections (IMIs), beyond this arbitrary cutoff. Current surveillance paradigms may fail to protect this vulnerable subgroup, leading to delayed diagnosis and poorer outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that allogeneic HSCT recipients with ongoing moderate-to-severe chronic GVHD requiring systemic immunosuppression beyond day 100 post-transplant constitute a persistently high-risk population for invasive mold infections, warranting extended antifungal prophylaxis and/or intensified diagnostic surveillance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, single-center cohort study of adult allogeneic HSCT recipients from 2015-2024. Using electronic health records, we will identify patients with chronic GVHD and extract data on IMI incidence, timing, and microbiological etiology (e.g., Aspergillus, Mucorales) up to 2 years post-HSCT. We will employ time-to-event analysis (Kaplan-Meier estimates, Cox proportional hazards models) to compare IMI risk before and after day 100. The models will adjust for key confounders including GVHD severity, steroid dose, lymphocyte count, and prior cytomegalovirus reactivation. A machine learning approach (LASSO regression) will be used to identify a parsimonious set of clinical factors predicting late-onset IMI.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, data-driven evidence to challenge the rigid 100-day surveillance window, directly informing clinical guideline updates for antifungal prophylaxis in high-risk HSCT populations. It will quantify the ongoing risk of IMIs associated with chronic GVHD and define a modern, risk-adapted timeline for post-transplant fungal infection prevention.
proposals.novelty: While late-onset IFIs are recognized, this study specifically links extended risk to the dynamic immunocompromised state of chronic GVHD management, moving beyond fixed timelines to a factor-driven surveillance model. It integrates Haste's expertise in post-transplant infection (e.g., vaccine response) with a focus on bacterial (MRSA/S. aureus) and viral (COVID-19) co-infections as potential competing risks or modifiers.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multicenter Validation of Galactomannan Screening for Early Detection and Preemptive Management of Invasive Aspergillosis in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Invasive aspergillosis (IA) remains a leading cause of infection-related mortality in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients. Despite the availability of the serum galactomannan (GM) assay, its role as a routine screening tool is debated due to variable reported performance and unclear cost-effectiveness, leading to inconsistent guidelines and clinical practice across centers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, twice-weekly serum GM screening protocol in the first 100 days post-HSCT will lead to earlier diagnosis of IA, prompt initiation of preemptive antifungal therapy, and a significant reduction in IA-related mortality compared to a strategy based on clinical suspicion alone.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, observational cohort study conducted across 10-15 major HSCT centers. All adult allogeneic HSCT recipients will undergo twice-weekly serum GM testing (Platelia™ Aspergillus Ag assay) for 100 days post-transplant. A predefined GM index threshold (≥0.5) will trigger standardized diagnostic workup (including chest CT) and preemptive antifungal therapy initiation. Primary outcomes will be IA-related mortality at day 180 and time-to-diagnosis. A parallel, detailed cost-effectiveness analysis will compare the screening strategy to standard care using a decision-analytic model incorporating direct medical costs and quality-adjusted life-years.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level, multicenter evidence to definitively establish the clinical utility and health-economic value of routine GM screening in this high-risk population. The results will directly inform international guidelines on antifungal prophylaxis and surveillance strategies, potentially standardizing care and improving outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While GM testing is known, this study's novelty lies in its prospective, multicenter design specifically powered to measure mortality impact and its integrated, rigorous cost-effectiveness analysis, which is lacking in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Schedule for Galactomannan Testing to Prevent Invasive Aspergillosis in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis
proposals.motivation: Invasive aspergillosis (IA) remains a leading cause of mortality in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) recipients. Serum galactomannan (GM) antigen testing is a cornerstone for early diagnosis, but its optimal application—balancing frequency, duration, and cost against clinical benefit—is poorly defined. Current guidelines are based on expert opinion, leading to variable and potentially inefficient practice in a high-cost, high-risk patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a risk-stratified, dynamic GM testing schedule, informed by patient-specific factors and post-transplant time, will be more cost-effective than a uniform, fixed-frequency protocol for the early detection of IA.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of allo-HSCT recipients from 2018-2025, using electronic health records to extract serial GM results, clinical outcomes, and cost data. A discrete-event simulation model will be developed to compare different testing strategies. The model will incorporate patient-level risk factors (e.g., graft-versus-host disease, neutropenia duration, corticosteroid use) and use reinforcement learning algorithms (specifically a Q-learning approach) to iteratively identify the testing policy that maximizes quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) while minimizing costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, quantitative framework for personalizing GM surveillance in allo-HSCT patients. It will deliver a clinically actionable testing algorithm that balances diagnostic yield with healthcare resource utilization. The findings will directly inform updates to antifungal stewardship and preventive care guidelines in transplant medicine.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have described IA risk factors, none have used simulation modeling coupled with machine learning to dynamically optimize the *schedule* of a specific diagnostic test. This moves beyond static risk prediction to prescribe a time-dependent testing strategy tailored to individual patient trajectories.

proposals.title: Impact of Mold-Active Antifungal Prophylaxis on the Diagnostic Performance of Serum Galactomannan Assay in High-Risk Hematology Patients
proposals.motivation: The serum galactomannan (GM) assay is a cornerstone for the non-invasive diagnosis of invasive aspergillosis (IA) in immunocompromised patients, such as allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients. However, its diagnostic utility may be compromised by the widespread use of mold-active antifungal prophylaxis (e.g., posaconazole, voriconazole, isavuconazole), which could suppress fungal antigenemia. Understanding this interaction is critical for accurate test interpretation and optimal patient management in the pre-2026 era of routine prophylaxis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of mold-active antifungal prophylaxis significantly reduces the sensitivity and alters the kinetics of serum GM positivity, thereby diminishing its negative predictive value for ruling out invasive aspergillosis in high-risk hematology patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients undergoing allogeneic HSCT or receiving intensive chemotherapy for acute leukemia at a major academic center from 2020-2025. Patients will be stratified by prophylactic regimen: posaconazole, voriconazole, isavuconazole, or no mold-active agent. Using prospectively collected biobanked sera, we will analyze serial GM levels (using the Platelia™ Aspergillus Ag assay) and correlate them with proven/probable IA outcomes per EORTC/MSG criteria. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to compare GM kinetics and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves to assess assay performance (sensitivity, specificity, NPV) under each prophylaxis condition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide clinicians with evidence-based guidance on how to interpret GM results in the context of specific antifungal prophylaxis, potentially leading to revised diagnostic algorithms. It will quantify the reduction in test performance, informing decisions on the need for supplemental diagnostic modalities (e.g., PCR, imaging) when prophylaxis is used.
proposals.novelty: While the suppressive effect of antifungals on GM is acknowledged, a direct, systematic comparison of the impact of different modern azole-based prophylaxis regimens on the quantitative performance and clinical utility of the GM assay in a real-world HSCT/leukemia cohort is lacking.

proposals.title: Modeling Aspergillus Galactomannan Kinetics to Predict Treatment Response and Guide Antifungal Therapy in High-Risk Hematology Patients
proposals.motivation: Invasive aspergillosis (IA) remains a leading cause of mortality in immunocompromised patients, such as those post-hematopoietic stem cell transplant. While serial serum galactomannan (GM) index measurement is a standard diagnostic tool, its quantitative kinetics are not formally used to monitor antifungal treatment efficacy in real-time. This represents a missed opportunity for personalized therapy, especially given the rise of azole-resistant Aspergillus and the need to optimize drug selection and duration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal decay pattern of serial serum GM measurements during antifungal therapy can be modeled to distinguish between treatment responders and non-responders earlier than clinical assessment, and that specific kinetic parameters correlate with microbiological eradication and patient survival.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of hematology patients with probable/proven IA (per EORTC/MSG criteria) treated between 2020-2025, with ≥3 serial GM measurements. We will fit individual patient GM time-series data to non-linear mixed-effects models (e.g., exponential decay) to extract kinetic parameters (e.g., half-life, clearance rate). Machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost) will be trained on these parameters, alongside clinical covariates (neutrophil recovery, antifungal drug), to predict 30-day treatment failure. Model performance will be validated using temporal cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for interpreting GM kinetics as a dynamic biomarker of antifungal treatment response. It will deliver a proof-of-concept predictive model that could later be integrated into clinical decision support systems to guide earlier therapy switches or escalation. The findings will advance the field beyond static diagnostic cut-offs towards a precision medicine approach for managing invasive fungal infections.
proposals.novelty: While GM is well-established for diagnosis, its systematic quantitative kinetic analysis for therapeutic monitoring is novel. This approach inverts the standard use of the biomarker, treating the serial measurements as an 'inverse problem' to infer in vivo antifungal pharmacodynamic efficacy, which has not been formally modeled in clinical cohorts.

proposals.title: Cross-Pathogen Investigation of Toxin-Mediated Platelet Clearance in Gram-Positive Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Severe thrombocytopenia is a life-threatening complication of bacteremia, linked to poor outcomes. Pre-2026 work, including from this group, has established that Staphylococcus aureus toxins like α-hemolysin can directly trigger platelet clearance, contributing to thrombocytopenia. It is unknown if this is a unique virulence strategy of S. aureus or a conserved pathogenic mechanism across other clinically significant Gram-positive pathogens, limiting our understanding of a common critical illness syndrome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pore-forming toxins produced by other Gram-positive pathogens (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus pyogenes, Bacillus cereus) will induce platelet activation, phosphatidylserine exposure, and accelerated clearance in a manner analogous to S. aureus α-hemolysin, contributing to thrombocytopenia during bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will use purified toxins (pneumolysin, streptolysin O, cereolysin O) and clinical bacteremia isolates to treat human platelets in vitro. Platelet activation (P-selectin exposure by flow cytometry), phosphatidylserine exposure (Annexin V binding), and aggregation will be quantified. A murine model of bacteremia will be employed for in vivo validation, infecting mice with wild-type and isogenic toxin-gene knockout strains of S. pneumoniae and S. pyogenes. Serial complete blood counts will track thrombocytopenia, and platelet lifespan will be measured via in vivo biotinylation and flow cytometric analysis of circulating platelets.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the broader relevance of a newly identified toxin-driven platelet clearance pathway beyond S. aureus. It will identify specific bacterial toxins as direct mediators of thrombocytopenia, offering new diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers. The findings could redirect therapeutic strategies toward toxin neutralization or platelet-protective agents for a wider range of bacterial infections.
proposals.novelty: While S. aureus toxin-platelet interactions are recognized, systematically testing this mechanism across other major pathogens is novel. The proposal moves from a pathogen-specific finding to a potentially unifying pathophysiological model for infection-associated thrombocytopenia.

proposals.title: A Phase IIb, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Ticagrelor as Adjunctive Therapy to Standard Antibiotics for Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia (SAB) remains a leading cause of sepsis and mortality, with MRSA posing a significant threat. Despite effective antibiotics, treatment failure rates are high due to bacterial virulence factors like α-toxin and the formation of platelet-bacterial aggregates that facilitate metastatic infection. The COVID-19 pandemic has further strained healthcare resources and highlighted the urgent need for novel, repurposed therapeutic strategies against severe bacterial infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Adjunctive therapy with the P2Y12 platelet inhibitor ticagrelor will attenuate S. aureus virulence by disrupting platelet-bacterial aggregation and α-toxin-mediated injury, thereby reducing the composite endpoint of mortality, persistent bacteremia, and metastatic complications compared to standard antibiotic therapy alone.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized controlled trial enrolling 120 adult patients with confirmed S. aureus bacteremia. Participants will be randomized 1:1 to receive oral ticagrelor (90 mg BID) or matched placebo for 14 days, in addition to standard-of-care antibiotics. The primary endpoint is a composite of all-cause mortality, persistent bacteremia at day 5, and new metastatic infectious complications within 30 days. Secondary endpoints include serial quantification of bacterial load, platelet activation markers (e.g., soluble P-selectin), and α-toxin activity in patient sera. Safety will be monitored via bleeding events (ISTH criteria). Statistical analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This trial will provide the first clinical evidence on the efficacy and safety of an antiplatelet agent as an antivirulence adjunct in SAB. It will establish proof-of-concept for targeting host-pathogen interactions, specifically platelet-mediated pathogenesis, as a therapeutic strategy. The findings could directly inform a larger Phase III trial and offer a rapidly deployable, repurposed therapy to improve outcomes in this severe infection.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Haste's prior work repurposed diflunisal as an antivirulence agent in vitro, this proposal translates the antivirulence concept into a clinical trial for bacteremia, targeting the host platelet response rather than bacterial adhesion proteins. It uniquely leverages pre-2026 mechanistic data on ticagrelor's inhibition of S. aureus α-toxin and platelet aggregation, a pathway distinct from the neuraminidase inhibition of oseltamivir.

ideas.title: Microbiome-Immune Reconstitution Profiling to Personalize Post-HCT Vaccine Timing and Infection Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to investigate whether the colonization versus true infection status of specific bacteria (e.g., Rahnella aquatilis, Burkholderia cepacia) in hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients can serve as a biomarker for functional immune reconstitution. By correlating these detailed microbiological states with adaptive immune responses to post-HCT vaccines administered at the standard three-month mark and at personalized timepoints, the study aims to determine if microbiome profiling can optimize vaccine timing. The goal is to move beyond a one-size-fits-all vaccination schedule and instead use an individual's unique microbiome-immune interface to predict the ideal moment for immunogenic and safe vaccine responses, thereby improving protection while minimizing infection risk.

ideas.title: Assess clinical outcomes like infection rates
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to evaluate if a similar immunologic response translates to equal protection against actual pneumococcal disease, analogous to the problem in structural engineering where laboratory material properties must be validated under real-world stress conditions. The engineering solution employs scaled prototypes and simulated environmental stress tests. Adapting this, the research would design a prospective, real-world validation study. Cohorts with quantified post-vaccination immunologic responses would be actively monitored in high-risk environments for the incidence of microbiologically confirmed pneumococcal disease (e.g., pneumonia, bacteremia), thereby directly testing the correlation between the surrogate biomarker (immune response) and the true clinical endpoint (disease protection).

ideas.title: Evaluate timing for other recommended vaccines
ideas.description: Investigate if the three-month timing is also effective for other post-HCT vaccines.
ideas.sourceTitle: Vaccine response after pneumococcal vaccination in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients

ideas.title: Breakthrough infection severity analysis
ideas.description: Analyze detailed clinical outcomes of post-T/C breakthrough infections beyond hospitalization.
ideas.sourceTitle: Real world effectiveness of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (Evusheld) in the Omicron era

ideas.title: Optimizing Post-HCT Vaccine Timing by Mapping Cellular Immune Reconstitution
ideas.description: This study will systematically evaluate the optimal timing for a panel of key post-hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) vaccines—including conjugate, polysaccharide, and live-attenuated vaccines—by longitudinally measuring antigen-specific T-cell (helper and memory subsets) and memory B-cell responses, in addition to antibody titers. By correlating these detailed cellular immune profiles with clinical outcomes, the research aims to identify distinct reconstitution milestones necessary for robust, durable responses to each vaccine type. This approach will generate a precision vaccination schedule tailored to the patient's recovering immune landscape, potentially improving efficacy and safety compared to current fixed-interval guidelines. The resulting data could redefine post-HCT vaccination as a biomarker-guided process rather than a calendar-based one.

ideas.title: Assess impact of antifungal prophylaxis on GM utility
ideas.description: Study how different mold-active antifungal prophylaxis regimens affect the performance and utility of the GM assay.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: PK/PD-Immune Modeling of Vaccine-Driven Immune Reconstitution in Transplant Recipients
ideas.description: This research fuses PK/PD modeling techniques from critical care with the investigation of vaccine-induced immune reconstitution. It will develop a mechanistic mathematical model that integrates the pharmacokinetics of vaccine components (e.g., mRNA, adjuvants) with pharmacodynamic outputs (e.g., antigen-specific T/B cell expansion) and their systemic immunomodulatory effects on recovering innate and adaptive immunity post-transplant. The model will be calibrated using longitudinal clinical data on vaccine responses, immune cell subsets, and infection rates. The goal is to generate predictive simulations to identify personalized vaccination schedules that maximize the rate of global immune recovery and provide protection against pathogens like SARS-CoV-2, beyond merely measuring vaccine immunogenicity.

ideas.title: Study impact of graft source and conditioning
ideas.description: Analyze how transplant characteristics influence vaccine response timing in subgroup analyses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Vaccine response after pneumococcal vaccination in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models to Optimize Post-HCT Vaccine Schedules
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using analogical reasoning from the field of ecology to refine the investigation of post-hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) vaccine timing. The analogous problem is ecological succession, where the sequence and timing of species reintroduction into a disturbed environment are critical for successful community restoration. The analogous solution involves mapping recovery stages and identifying key environmental parameters that signal readiness for each successive species. Adapted to immunology, the research would conceptualize the post-HCT immune system as a disturbed ecosystem. The goal is to move beyond a one-size-fits-all three-month benchmark. Instead, the study would define distinct, measurable stages of immune reconstitution and identify specific immunological biomarkers that signal the optimal, pathogen-specific timing for administering other recommended vaccines (e.g., pneumococcal, HPV, influenza), thereby creating a staged, personalized vaccine schedule based on the patient's unique 'immune recovery trajectory'.

ideas.title: Relax the need for positive culture for diagnosis
ideas.description: Assess bacterial pneumonia prevalence using clinical, radiological, and biomarker criteria without requiring positive cultures.
ideas.sourceTitle: 374. Complication with Bacterial Pneumonia in Hospitalized Patients with Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19)

ideas.title: Metagenomic Profiling of the Respiratory Resistome to Predict Pneumococcal Disease Risk and Vaccine Efficacy
ideas.description: This research applies deep shotgun metagenomic sequencing to serial respiratory samples from a cohort undergoing pneumococcal vaccination, such as older adults or high-risk patients. It moves beyond cataloging pathogens to longitudinally track the dynamics of the entire respiratory microbiome and resistome in response to vaccination. By correlating these high-resolution microbial profiles with prospective, rigorously adjudicated clinical outcomes of pneumococcal disease (e.g., pneumonia, invasive disease), the study aims to identify which pre-existing or emergent bacterial strains and resistance gene constellations are associated with vaccine failure or breakthrough infection. This approach directly tests the hypothesis that vaccine efficacy is mediated not just by host immunity but by the ecological and genetic structure of the resident respiratory microbial community.

ideas.title: Challenge that six months yields more robust response
ideas.description: Test if earlier vaccination at three months is non-inferior for all key clinical outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Vaccine response after pneumococcal vaccination in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients

ideas.title: Assess combination therapy with standard antibiotics
ideas.description: Evaluate diflunisal's adjunctive benefit with specific antibiotics in resistant S. aureus infections.
ideas.sourceTitle: Repurposing Diflunisal as an Antivirulence Agent Against Staphylococcus aureus

ideas.title: Causal modeling of transplant characteristics on time-to-vaccine response using targeted learning
ideas.description: This study employs a causal inference framework to estimate the direct effect of graft source (e.g., matched unrelated donor vs. haploidentical) and conditioning intensity on the time-to-seroconversion following a defined vaccine schedule in allo-HCT recipients. The primary analysis pre-specifies a statistical model using Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) to adjust for a core set of time-varying confounders, including immunosuppressive drug levels and incident GVHD, measured at protocol-defined intervals before each vaccine dose. This design moves beyond associative subgroup analyses to provide robust, adjusted effect estimates for each transplant characteristic, with sensitivity analyses quantifying how strong unmeasured confounding would need to be to nullify the observed effects.

ideas.title: Relax the 100-day post-HSCT surveillance window
ideas.description: Extend GM surveillance beyond 100 days post-transplant for patients with ongoing high-risk factors like GVHD.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: Invert the empiric intervention paradigm: A trial of delayed vaccination and restricted antibiotics guided by host-response diagnostics
ideas.description: This study inverts the default paradigm of early, empiric intervention by testing a synergistic protocol of delayed routine vaccination (beyond 6 months) combined with highly restricted empiric antibiotic use. The core innovation is using rapid host-response diagnostics (e.g., transcriptomic signatures) to guide both decisions: delaying vaccination until a mature immune phenotype is detected, and withholding antibiotics for suspected infections unless a severe bacterial host-response signature is confirmed. This tests the hypothesis that reducing early medical interventions in infancy, when guided by precise physiological monitoring, allows for more robust natural immune development and reduces microbiome disruption, potentially leading to lower rates of immune-mediated and metabolic diseases later in childhood.

ideas.title: In vitro kinetics directly predict in vivo efficacy
ideas.description: Challenge the translational assumption by testing compounds in complex infection models early.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bactericidal Kinetics of Marine-Derived Napyradiomycins against Contemporary Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus

ideas.title: Assess impact on other bacterial pathogens
ideas.description: Investigate if the toxin-driven platelet clearance pathway is relevant in bacteremia caused by other pathogens.
ideas.sourceTitle: Repurposed drugs block toxin-driven platelet clearance by the hepatic Ashwell-Morell receptor to clear Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia

ideas.title: Test 6-APA combination therapy in vivo
ideas.description: Evaluate the efficacy of subinhibitory 6-aminopenicillanic acid combined with cathelicidin or other innate immune factors in animal infection models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Beta-Lactamase Repressor BlaI Modulates Staphylococcus aureus Cathelicidin Antimicrobial Peptide Resistance and Virulence

ideas.title: Stratify by risk to optimize timing: Identifying patients for whom very early vaccination uniquely prevents bacterial pneumonia complications
ideas.description: This research investigates whether patients who derive unique, outsized benefit from very early COVID-19 vaccination constitute the same population at intrinsically lowest risk for secondary bacterial pneumonia. By analyzing linked data on vaccination timing, host immune responses, and infection outcomes, the goal is to define a high-priority subgroup for accelerated vaccination *and* a corresponding low-risk subgroup for antibiotic stewardship. This creates a unified predictive model where an individual's potential gain from ultra-early prevention directly informs their risk profile for subsequent complications, guiding two critical decisions in the care pathway. The synergy lies in using a single stratification to simultaneously personalize preventive strategy and therapeutic restraint, moving beyond uniform guidelines.

ideas.title: Stratified Immunological Readiness: A Confounder-Aware Framework for Evaluating Vaccination Timing
ideas.description: This study investigates whether delaying vaccination past the standard schedule can be beneficial for specific, well-defined patient subgroups, while rigorously controlling for the confounding factors that make delay appear spuriously advantageous. It employs a multi-arm prospective cohort design comparing outcomes between patients on the standard schedule, those with a medically advised delay based on predefined immunological or clinical criteria, and those with purely administrative delays. The analysis integrates high-frequency immune monitoring, detailed clinical metadata, and real-time local pathogen circulation data to model the interaction between delay timing, individual immune readiness, and community risk. The goal is to generate actionable, strata-specific guidance that identifies which patients, if any, might genuinely benefit from a later vaccination, and under what precise epidemiological conditions.

ideas.title: Prospective, randomized non-inferiority trial with comprehensive immunological profiling comparing three- versus six-month vaccine intervals
ideas.description: This trial will randomize participants to receive a vaccine booster at either a three- or six-month interval. The primary objective is to establish the non-inferiority of the shorter interval based on a composite endpoint of antibody titers at 12 months post-booster, using a pre-defined, clinically justified margin. Secondary and exploratory endpoints will include detailed assessments of cellular immunity, antibody avidity, and cross-reactive responses against variant strains. The study will be explicitly powered to detect meaningful differences in these secondary parameters and will include pre-specified subgroup analyses (e.g., by age, prior infection status) to ensure the findings are robust and generalizable.

ideas.title: Test diflunisal against other pathogens with similar regulators
ideas.description: Explore diflunisal's antivirulence potential in bacteria with AgrA-homologous response regulators.
ideas.sourceTitle: Repurposing Diflunisal as an Antivirulence Agent Against Staphylococcus aureus

ideas.title: Comparative effectiveness with other prophylactic options
ideas.description: Compare T/C's real-world outcomes against other pre-exposure prophylactics like bebtelovimab or oral antivirals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Real world effectiveness of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (Evusheld) in the Omicron era

ideas.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Culture-Independent Diagnosis and Phenotyping of Bacterial Pneumonia in COVID-19
ideas.description: This research synthesizes a machine learning approach, leveraging models pre-trained on sepsis for their sensitivity to early systemic infection signals, with a clinical diagnostic framework that does not require positive microbiological cultures. The core innovation is developing and validating an integrated ML model that uses early, routinely available data—including lab trends, chest imaging reports, and host-response biomarkers like procalcitonin—to generate a probabilistic, culture-independent diagnosis of bacterial pneumonia in COVID-19 patients. This enables the precise stratification of patients for antimicrobial stewardship trials and the study of bacterial co-infection epidemiology, addressing a critical evidence gap created by the low sensitivity of cultures in this population.

ideas.title: Assume T/C offers no protection
ideas.description: Test if observed hospitalization reduction is due to prior infection immunity, not T/C.
ideas.sourceTitle: Real world effectiveness of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (Evusheld) in the Omicron era

ideas.title: Testing the 'Critical Period' Assumption in Infant Vaccination by Analogy to Plant Hardening
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that six months is the optimal or minimal age for a robust vaccine response by drawing an analogy from plant physiology. In horticulture, the traditional assumption that seedlings must be mature before hardening was overturned by research showing earlier, controlled stress induces systemic acquired resistance (priming). The analogous solution is adapted by proposing a clinical trial to test if vaccination at three months acts as an 'immune hardening' event. The adapted research idea is to design a non-inferiority trial comparing key clinical outcomes (e.g., disease incidence, antibody titers, cell-mediated immunity) in infants vaccinated at three months versus the standard six-month schedule, with the hypothesis that earlier vaccination may prime the immune system for a robust, non-inferior, or superior long-term response.

ideas.title: Clinical trial for bacteremia repurposing
ideas.description: Design a clinical trial to repurpose ticagrelor or oseltamivir as adjunctive therapy for S. aureus bacteremia.
ideas.sourceTitle: Repurposed drugs block toxin-driven platelet clearance by the hepatic Ashwell-Morell receptor to clear Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia

ideas.title: Adaptive, Mechanism-Informed PK/PD Modeling for Antibiotic Dosing in COVID-19 Pneumonia Using Real-Time Host-Response Data
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic, adaptive pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling platform specifically for antibiotic treatment of bacterial pneumonia in COVID-19 patients. Instead of applying static ICU models, it integrates continuous streams of patient-specific data—including inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6), markers of endothelial dysfunction, and bacterial load—to inform a mechanism-based model of drug disposition and effect. The system employs a Bayesian learning framework to individualize and continuously adjust dosing recommendations in real-time, accounting for the evolving host-pathogen-drug triad. This approach aims to optimize antibiotic exposure by directly addressing the unique and non-stationary pathophysiology of COVID-19, thereby improving treatment efficacy and mitigating resistance development.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: GM clearance as treatment monitor
ideas.description: Use serial GM measurements to monitor antifungal treatment efficacy, not just for initial diagnosis.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: Prospective multicenter validation of GM screening utility
ideas.description: A prospective, multi-center study is needed to validate the utility and cost-effectiveness of routine GM screening in HSCT patients.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: Vaccination as a 'Keystone Intervention' for Immune System Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecological restoration, where introducing a keystone species catalyzes the recovery of an entire degraded ecosystem. The analogous problem is immune system degradation and slow reconstitution following hematopoietic stem cell transplant. The analogous solution is the targeted introduction of a keystone element to trigger system-wide recovery. Adapted to this context, the study will investigate if vaccination itself acts as a 'keystone immunological intervention.' It will test the hypothesis that certain vaccines, through mechanisms like bystander T-cell activation, innate immune training, or providing a structured antigenic challenge, can positively influence the pace and quality of global immune reconstitution post-transplant, thereby scaling the immune system's recovery in a dual manner—specific protection and broader systemic enhancement.

ideas.title: Use time-series analysis from econometrics
ideas.description: Apply interrupted time-series analysis to model infection trends before and after T/C administration.
ideas.sourceTitle: Real world effectiveness of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (Evusheld) in the Omicron era

ideas.title: Single-Cell Immune Profiling to Validate and Refine Culture-Independent Diagnostic Criteria for Bacterial Pneumonia
ideas.description: This research program integrates a clinical diagnostic protocol (using clinical, radiological, and biomarker criteria) with deep single-cell RNA sequencing of respiratory and blood samples from the same patients. The goal is to map the comprehensive host immune landscape—including dysfunctional myeloid cells, depleted T cell states, and altered epithelial responses—that underlies cases meeting the culture-independent criteria for bacterial pneumonia. This enables the replacement of broad syndromic labels with precise, biologically defined 'immune permissiveness' signatures. Ultimately, these mechanistically grounded signatures can be translated back into refined, simpler clinical algorithms, creating a pathophysiology-informed diagnostic standard that does not rely on insensitive microbiological cultures.

ideas.title: Anti-inflammatory effect is separable from antivirulence
ideas.description: Determine if diflunisal's host-directed anti-inflammatory action confounds infection model outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Repurposing Diflunisal as an Antivirulence Agent Against Staphylococcus aureus

ideas.title: Invert: Treat based on GM without confirmatory workup
ideas.description: Investigate outcomes of preemptive antifungal therapy triggered solely by GM positivity, bypassing confirmatory diagnostics.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: Adapt pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling from ICU
ideas.description: Model antibiotic dosing in COVID-19 patients with altered physiology to optimize bacterial pneumonia treatment.
ideas.sourceTitle: 374. Complication with Bacterial Pneumonia in Hospitalized Patients with Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19)

ideas.title: Scale down to individual biomarker level
ideas.description: Correlate post-T/C antibody titers or cellular immunity markers with breakthrough infection risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Real world effectiveness of tixagevimab/cilgavimab (Evusheld) in the Omicron era

ideas.title: A Prospective Surveillance and Predictive Modeling Framework for Real-Time and Forecasted Vaccine Effectiveness Against Continuously Evolving Omicron Lineages
ideas.description: This study establishes a multi-national, linked genomic and health record cohort to assess T/C effectiveness against currently circulating Omicron subvariants with minimal latency. Concurrently, it develops and validates a predictive model that integrates real-world vaccine effectiveness (VE) estimates, longitudinal serological neutralization data against a broad panel of variants, and global viral sequence evolution trends. The output is a continuously updated forecast of VE against newly detected and phylogenetically projected subvariants, transforming the research from a historical record into a tool for proactive public health decision-making.

ideas.title: Define optimal GM testing frequency and duration
ideas.description: Research should determine the most cost-effective schedule for GM testing, including frequency and duration post-transplant.
ideas.sourceTitle: 2055. Utility of Aspergillus Galactomannan Assay in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients

ideas.title: Assess impact of empiric antibiotics on outcomes using a 'pre-emptive vs. targeted' framework from precision agriculture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from precision agriculture, where the efficacy and consequences of blanket pre-emptive interventions are compared to targeted, need-based applications. In agriculture, this is done via controlled trials with distinct treatment zones and sensor-based outcome mapping. Adapted to clinical research, the study would compare patient cohorts receiving empiric (broad-spectrum, pre-emptive) antibiotics versus targeted (narrow-spectrum, pathogen-directed) therapy. The solution involves using dense, longitudinal clinical data as high-resolution sensors to measure and compare multi-dimensional outcome trajectories, including speed of clinical recovery, incidence of adverse ecological effects (like C. difficile infection or antimicrobial resistance selection), and overall healthcare resource use, providing a holistic assessment of the intervention's impact.

ideas.title: Emulating a Target Trial to Estimate the Causal Effect of Empiric vs. Culture-Guided Antibiotic Strategy on Clinical Outcomes
ideas.description: This study employs a causal inference and target trial emulation framework to rigorously estimate the effect of initial antibiotic strategy. Using detailed electronic health record data, it defines a cohort at the moment a microbiological culture is ordered, emulating randomization. Advanced methods (e.g., high-dimensional propensity score matching) are used to balance measured confounders like illness severity, infection source, and comorbidities between patients who empirically receive antibiotics covering the eventual pathogen versus those who do not. The primary analysis compares time-to-clinical failure (a composite of death, ICU transfer, or antibiotic escalation) from this uniform time-zero, providing a less biased estimate of the strategy's impact.

ideas.title: Differentiating Pathogen from Passenger: A Host-Response and Contextual Burden Diagnostic Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all detected bacteria in a clinical sample are pathogenic by drawing an analogy from astronomy's exoplanet detection field. In exoplanet searches, a single observational signal is insufficient to confirm a planet; astronomers use multiple detection methods and contextual data to rule out false positives. The adapted solution is to create a clinical diagnostic framework that similarly refuses to equate bacterial presence with disease. Instead, it integrates three core data streams: 1) the microbial signal (species identity, genomic virulence factors), 2) the host response signal (patient-specific immune biomarkers indicating active infection), and 3) the contextual burden (quantitative bacterial load and clinical metadata). By synthesizing these signals, the framework aims to probabilistically differentiate true infection from colonization for organisms like Rahnella aquatilis or Burkholderia cepacia, reducing unnecessary treatments and improving patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Analyze risk factors for specific causative organisms
ideas.description: Investigate if different patient factors predispose to Staphylococcus aureus versus Enterobacteriaceae pneumonia.
ideas.sourceTitle: 374. Complication with Bacterial Pneumonia in Hospitalized Patients with Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19)

ideas.title: Antigenic Cartography for Predicting Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab (T/C) Effectiveness Against Future Omicron Subvariants
ideas.description: This study adapts the solution to the analogous problem of predicting influenza vaccine effectiveness against drifting strains—solved via antigenic cartography—to the challenge of forecasting Tixagevimab/Cilgavimab (T/C) effectiveness against later Omicron subvariants like BA.5 and BQ.1. The analogous solution from influenza virology involves using serological assay data to computationally map the antigenic relationships between viruses, visualizing evolutionary drift. We adapt this by performing high-throughput neutralization assays with T/C against a broad panel of SARS-CoV-2 variants, from the original strain through Omicron sub-lineages. The resulting antigenic map will quantify how far subsequent subvariants have drifted from the therapeutic's target epitopes. This model can then be used to predict, in near real-time, the likely clinical effectiveness of T/C against newly emerging variants based on their genetic sequence and preliminary neutralization data, providing a tool for proactive clinical guidance and therapeutic development.

ideas.title: A Probabilistic, Etiology-Focused Syndromic Classifier for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a multi-modal diagnostic framework for community-acquired pneumonia that moves beyond the binary 'culture-positive/negative' paradigm. It integrates clinical data, chest imaging patterns, and a panel of host-response biomarkers into a machine learning classifier that outputs a probabilistic etiology likelihood (e.g., 'high probability typical bacterial', 'probable viral', 'indeterminate/atypical'). The system is trained and tested against an expanded reference standard incorporating multiplex PCR, antigen testing, and paired serology to ensure phenotypic clarity. The goal is to provide clinicians with a more nuanced, actionable diagnosis that directly informs targeted therapy and antibiotic stewardship decisions.

ideas.title: Systemic infection is the primary model

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Predict Antibiotic Efficacy in COVID-19 Patients
ideas.description: This idea expands the original PK/PD modeling concept by drawing an analogy from ecology. In conservation biology, species distribution models (SDMs) predict an organism's range by analyzing its presence data against environmental variables. The analogous solution is to treat the complex, altered physiology of a severe COVID-19 patient (e.g., hyperinflammation, renal/hepatic dysfunction, capillary leak) as a dynamic 'environmental landscape.' The adapted research idea is to model antibiotic efficacy not just on traditional PK/PD curves, but by correlating bacterial kill rates with this multivariate host environment. By adapting niche modeling algorithms, we can predict the 'therapeutic niche'—the specific combination of drug concentration and patient physiology required for optimal treatment of secondary bacterial pneumonia, enabling real-time, personalized dose adjustment.

ideas.title: Applying Immunological 'Prime-Boost' Timing Strategy to Infant Vaccination
ideas.description: This idea inverts the primary timing question in vaccinology by challenging the assumption that earlier is always better for all patients. Drawing an analogy from cancer immunotherapy, where delaying booster T-cell infusions prevents exhaustion and improves outcomes, we adapt this 'strategic delay' solution. The adapted research idea proposes a clinical trial to test if deliberately delaying certain infant vaccine doses beyond the standard six-month timeline—to coincide with a more mature immune system—could yield superior long-term immune memory and protection in identifiable patient subgroups, thereby personalizing vaccination timing based on immune maturation rather than chronological age alone.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of uniform patient population
